Name: 34_disaster_centers_response_outages
Representative_Docs: ['Disaster response — Information on disaster response is available from the American Red Cross in English and Spanish .', "SBA's Disaster Field Operations Centers are responsible for standing up local business recovery centers and disaster loan outreach centers, as well as staffing federal and state disaster recovery centers in disaster sites. These field offices act as one-stop-shops for businesses, homeowners, and renters seeking to cover losses caused by declared natural disasters.", 'Several government agencies offer online disaster information Food safety after power outages — Information is available from the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services . Disaster response — Information on disaster response is available from the American Red Cross in English and Spanish .']

Name: 35_rescue_department_form_smoke
Representative_Docs: ["Home FAQ Frequently Asked Questions Below you will find information that might help you understand how to find things or learn about information you might need to know about your city or town. Fire-Rescue Department 5 All Content Does your department check and install car seats for children? Yes! Fill out the Child Passenger Safety Inspection Request form to schedule an appointment, or visit our Child Car Seat Installation page for more information. Fire-Rescue Department How do I order a reflective address sign? Reflective address signs are very important for emergency services. If we can't find you, we can't help you. Locating addresses can be especially tricky at night. Order a reflective address sign today. Signs are only $15 or $20 if mounted to a metal post. Use this form to order your reflective address sign. The signs are made at the fire-rescue department and you will be contacted once it is ready for pick-up. Fire-Rescue Department Does your department check and install smoke alarms and/or carbon monoxide detectors? As a part of our Community Risk Reduction program the Fire-Rescue Department installs smoke and carbon monoxide alarms for residents of Carrboro and the South Orange Fire District. Smoke alarms provide early notifications increasing the likelihood that you and your family will be able to escape if there is a fire in your home. Submit this form to schedule installation or inspection of a smoke or carbon monoxide alarm. Fire-Rescue Department How can I apply for a fire prevention permit? The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department issues construction and operational permits in accordance with applicable state laws and the currently adopted edition of the North Carolina Fire Code. For more information regarding permits, reference Section 105 of the North Carolina Fire Code and in Chapter 12, Article II, Section 12-10 of the Town Code. You can apply for a fire prevention permit by submitting this form . Reminder: Any plans for review (electrical, remodeling, fire alarm, sprinkler system) should be submitted to Town of Carrboro Planning, Zoning, and Inspections through PIPER prior to contacting the Fire-Rescue Department. Fire-Rescue Department How to I schedule a fire prevention education event or fire station tour? Birthday Party? Other events? Public Education Event We provide public education events, including fire prevention presentations. Use this form to request a public education event for your group or organization. Station Tours We regularly give station tours and safety talks to all types of groups, both formal and informal. Some of the groups we have had come through in the past include school classes, girl scouts, boy scouts, church groups, day care groups, after school programs, summer camp groups, home school students, and many individuals. Use this form to request a fire station tour. Birthday and Block Parties If you are planning a community block party or birthday party our department will stop by during the event with a fire truck for all to see up close - both adults and children. We can show and explain the use and importance of the many pieces of equipment, display our gear, discuss fire safety topics, and distribute fire safety information. Use this form to request the department come out to your birthday or block party. Other Events View our Community Services page to see what other services are offered by the fire-rescue department. Name: 37_damage_apartment_reported_heavy
Representative_Docs: ['Carrboro Fire-Rescue Responds to Structure Fire Shortly after noon Wednesday, Feb. 21, members of the Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department responded to 10 Alston Drive for a reported structure fire. There were no injuries reported at the fire. \xa0Fire damaged three outbuildings, as well as additional equipment and a wooded area in the vicinity of the structures. Initial damage estimates are approximately $65,000. Responding crews reported heavy smoke visible while approaching the scene. The first crew arrived in just over six minutes and found multiple outbuildings on fire along with other equipment. The fire had spread to the wooded area surrounding the buildings as well. Fire crews immediately initiated a fire attack and extinguished the fire in approximately 16 minutes. The cause of the fire is under investigation by the Orange County Emergency Services Fire and Life Division. Alston Drive is in an unincorporated area of Orange County. It is in the South Orange Fire District and is protected by the Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department. The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department received assistance from the Chapel Hill Fire Department, White Cross Fire Department, North Chatham Fire Department, Orange County Emergency Medical Services, and the Orange County Sherriff’s Office.', 'Fire damages Carrboro Apartment In the early morning hours of Wednesday, March 26, members of the Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department responded to Berkshire 54 Apartments located at 101 NC 54 Bypass for a reported structure fire. Additional information was given that heavy smoke and flames were visible. Responding crews arrived in about five minutes and found an apartment building with flames visible in one apartment and the breezeway. Fire crews immediately initiated a fire attack and extinguished the fire in about 20 minutes. Fire damage was contained to one apartment. There was smoke damage in the breezeway. Initial damage estimates are $75,000. There were no injuries reported at the fire. Two residents were displaced from one apartment, which was deemed uninhabitable. They are being assisted by friends and property management. The cause of the fire is under investigation. A total of 23 firefighters responded to the fire. The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department received assistance from the Chapel Hill Fire Department, White Cross Fire Department, Orange Grove Fire Department, Orange County Emergency Medical Services, and the Carrboro Police Department. The New Hope Fire Department assisted with coverage during the fire.', 'CARRBORO, NC – On Thursday, April 16, 2026, at approximately 2 a.m. members of the Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department responded to 601 Jones Ferry Road for a reported structure fire. Initial reports indicated heavy smoke and visible flames. Responding crews arrived in just over four minutes and encountered heavy smoke and fire conditions on the second and third floors of a multi-unit apartment building. Due to the volume of fire and the need for additional personnel, a second alarm was requested to bring additional firefighters to the scene. Firefighters initiated an aggressive interior fire attack, and the fire was brought under control within approximately 60 minutes. Multiple units sustained significant fire damage, with additional smoke damage to adjacent units. A total of 18 residents were displaced. The property management group and the American Red Cross are assisting displaced residents with temporary housing and immediate needs. One resident was transported with minor injuries. One firefighter was evaluated on scene for minor injuries. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. Preliminary Damage Estimate: $400,000 The Carrboro Fire-Rescue Department received assistance from the Chapel Hill Fire Department, White Cross Fire Department, North Chatham Fire Department, Orange Grove Fire Department, New Hope Fire Department, and the Eno Fire Department. Orange County Emergency Services and the Carrboro Police Department also provided support. ### Related News Town Council Update Carrboro Town News Posted on June 18, 2026 Town Council Update Carrboro Town News Posted on June 12, 2026 Carrboro Celebrates Juneteenth Holiday Carrboro Town News Posted on June 12, 2026 Carrboro Art Exhibitions Now Showing at the Drakeford Library Complex and Carrboro Town Hall Carrboro Town News Posted on May 06, 2026 Town of Carrboro Selects Latasha “Tasha” Pegues as Human Resources Director Carrboro Town News Posted on June 09, 2026']

Name: 38_invasive_species_native_spread
Representative_Docs: ['Watch our Black History Month Recap. Read the Town Council Update. Learn how you can prevent the spread of invasive species to the places that you love during National Invasive Species Awareness Week. Check out the events coming this weekend including Carrboro in Motion and Florafitti. All these and more in Carrboro This Week!', '“Raising awareness and educating others about prevention of the growth and spread of invasive species is an important part of Carrboro’s Community Climate Action Plan since the spread of non-native and invasive plants continue to be a threat to forest and biodiversity sustainability,” Mayor Foushee said.', 'National Invasive Species Awareness Week Mayor Barbara Foushee has proclaimed Feb. 26 to March 3 National Invasive Species Awareness Week in Carrboro. Join the Town of Carrboro’s efforts to make our local ecosystems more resilient to the effects of climate change and other biological challenges. “Raising awareness and educating others about prevention of the growth and spread of invasive species is an important part of Carrboro’s Community Climate Action Plan since the spread of non-native and invasive plants continue to be a threat to forest and biodiversity sustainability,” Mayor Foushee said. The term "invasive" is used for non-native species that spread and reproduce rapidly, displace native species, and cause major disturbance to the areas in which they are present. Invasive species can reduce biodiversity, threaten the survival of native plants and animals, and interfere with ecosystem functions. How can you prevent the spread of invasive species to the places that you love? The good news is that solutions start at home. Here’s how: Learn to identify and remove invasive species in your yard or neighborhood using one of the following resources: Online Guides Invasive Plants of Carrboro Presentation iNaturalist Phone App Seek Phone App Take extra care when traveling, gardening, or moving plant material. Buy your plants from a reputable source and plant native species to support your local ecosystem. Volunteer to help remove invasive species from public lands and natural areas. Town of Carrboro Stormwater Volunteer Form All week long you can join Lieutenant Leif on Town’s social media and website to learn about six commonly found invasive plant species in Carrboro. Learn more about Carrboro’s commitment to climate action and protecting and enhancing local ecosystems in the Community Climate Action Plan and Comprehensive Plan . Read the full National Invasive Species Awareness Week Proclamation .']

Name: 40_website_redesign_features_government
Representative_Docs: ['CARRBORO, NC – The Town of Carrboro is pleased to announce that it has launched a website redesign that streamlines the homepage with commonly accessed pages, useable and relevant features, and improves accessibility for all users. The website at www.carrboronc.gov just underwent a redesign process by CivicPlus, the Town’s government website provider. The website now has improved functionality as well as a cleaner, easier layout to navigate. In addition to the primary government website, the Town of Carrboro offers an engagement platform at https://engage.carrboronc.gov/ ; “A modern, mobile-friendly, and intuitive site enables faster access to services, reduces administrative costs, and meets the high expectations of our residents for online interaction,” said Town Manager Patrice Toney. “Our efforts at modernizing online platforms are essential for fostering trust, ensuring legal compliance, and improving operational efficiency.” Residents and their local government will be able to access more information and more easily through some of the new featured modules: Improved calendar function that makes it easy to toggle between meetings and events Government area now features links to Town Pillars: Race and Equity and Climate Action Streamlines commonly accessed links under main categories including a new Projects area to provide status updates on town initiatives and improvements underway Provides prominent Translation function Includes a regular video feature Adds social media content to the homepage Complies with ADA standards for accessibility with more features to be added soon With the new website, the Town of Carrboro will increase its community engagement and enhance the communication between residents and local government. Name: 41_poetry_poets_laureate_poet
Representative_Docs: ['Home Culture Arts and Culture Poetry In Carrboro West End Poetry Festival', 'Since 2006, many poets from North Carolina and beyond have participated. Participants have included finalists for the National Book Award and the Pulitzer Prize; winners of the Kingsley Tufts Award, the Los Angeles Book Prize, the Oregon Book Award, and the Pushcart Prize; recipients of fellowships Guggenheim Foundation and the National Endowment for the Arts; and too many other accolades to easily list here.', 'Many of the town’s community partners vital to programming these events joined us at the holiday parade – Liza Wolff-Francis, our wonderful outgoing poet laureate who has put on the West End Poetry Festival, Bryan Reklis who is gearing up for the Carrboro Film Fest in January, and Mauricio Solano of El Centro Hispano who helped put on Fiestas en la Calle: Festival LatinoAmericano as well as partner with the town on the Carrboro Orgullo Pride Celebration. In addition, the parade troupe featured Josephus Thompson III, the poet laureate for the City of Greensboro who was a featured presenter at a past West End Poetry Festival; and Rayah Charles, a singer who performed at the 2024 Juneteenth Celebration.']

Name: 43_met_tuesday_hall_took
Representative_Docs: ['The Carrboro Town Council met on Tuesday, March 17 and took the following actions:', 'The Carrboro Town Council met on Tuesday, March 10 and took the following actions:', 'TOWN HALL set for Saturday, March 29 at the Drakeford Library Complex The Town of Carrboro will host a Town Hall meeting at 11 a.m. Saturday, March 29, to give residents an opportunity to express their thoughts in the wake of enacted or proposed changes across various policy areas at state and federal levels of government. The Town Hall will take place in the theater/performance space at the Drakeford Library Complex, 203 S. Greensboro St. Residents are encouraged to attend to share suggestions, questions and concerns. For several weeks, residents from across town have been reaching out informally to share their concerns over impacts from the reshaping of federal government. There are also changes proposed from the state level. NC General Assembly House Bill 244 would allow only “certain flags” to be flown or displayed on state and local government properties. House Bill 171 would prohibit the use of government funds in support of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) programs in state and local governments. The bill includes civil and criminal penalties for noncompliance, including a Class I misdemeanor offense. At the Town Hall, Town Manager Patrice Toney will share actions the town is taking to prepare for policies coming down from higher levels of government. This is a valuable opportunity for residents to make their voices heard and actively participate in a public forum. The Town Hall will be videoed and posted to Town of Carrboro Facebook and YouTube channels. For more information, contact the Communication and Engagement Department at info@carrboronc.gov']

Name: 44_immigrant_communities_rights_4th
Representative_Docs: ['Home News Flash Carrboro Town Council Voices Support for Immigrant Communities and 4th Amendment Rights', "Carrboro Town Council expresses support for immigrant communities The Town of Carrboro recognizes the vital role that immigrants have played and continue to play in the growth, diversity and prosperity of our community. In a resolution passed at its March 19, 2024 meeting, the Carrboro Town Council resolves to: Commit to fostering a welcoming environment for all immigrants living in, working in and contributing to our town. Advocate for policies at the state and federal level that provide a clear path to citizenship for undocumented residents and protect the rights of refugees and asylees. Support local initiatives to provide all residents with access to municipal services, including education and law enforcement protection, regardless of immigration status. Empower and encourage law enforcement officials to intentionally foster trust and cooperation with our diverse immigrant communities. Encourage the community to engage in dialogue and cultural exchange to bolster mutual understanding and respect among all residents of Carrboro. Photo caption: (l-r) Carrboro Town Council Member Eliazar Posada with N.C. Rep. Ricky Hurtado of Mebane (District 63 in Alamance County), chair of the Governor's Advisory Council on Hispanic/Latino Affairs. Read the full resolution here .", 'Carrboro Town Council Voices Support for Immigrant Communities and 4th Amendment Rights']

Name: 49_venable_today_carr_railroad
Representative_Docs: ['Home Culture About Carrboro History of Carrboro History of Carrboro Carrboro was first settled in 1882 around a University of North Carolina railroad spur. It was originally known as West End, due to its geographic location directly west of Chapel Hill. In 1911, the town was incorporated under the name Venable, for chemistry professor and University of North Carolina president Francis Preston Venable. It wasn’t until 1913 that the town made its final name change after Julian Shakespeare Carr, owner of the local textile mill, after Carr expanded the mill and agreed to provide electricity to the community in exchange for naming the town after himself. For the first fifty years after its incorporation, Carrboro remained a small mill town with a slow, steady pace of growth. In 1960, approximately 2,000 people lived in the town. In the late 1960s the town’s population began to increase stemming from the growth occurring at UNC-Chapel Hill and area businesses. Also during the late 1960s, Carrboro began to become more progressive in its thinking. Today the town has a reputation as one of the most progressive communities in the South. Carrboro was the first municipality in North Carolina to elect an openly gay Mayor in 1995, and was also the first in the state to grant domestic-partner benefits to same-sex couples. Growth has continued through the 1980s, 1990s, and 2000s. This has resulted in the creation of a vibrant and diverse community. Carrboro Farmer’s Market, two venues that host national music acts (the ArtsCenter and Cat’s Cradle) call Carrboro home. The town also hosts annual events like the Carrboro Music Festival and Carrboro Film Festival. Today, over 20,000 people are able to call Carrboro home. Carrboro’s roots began in the late 19 th century when a branch of the North Carolina Railroad extended south to the edge of Chapel Hill, and the first local textile mill opened nearby. Informally known as West End and Lloydville, the community incorporated as town named Venable in 1911. Two years later, the state legislature renamed the town Carrboro at request of Julian S. Carr, a post-Civil War business leader. He was also an active and influential participant in Jim Crow era efforts to create a system of racial segregation. Although the town continues to bear his name, the values and actions of Carr do not represent Carrboro today. In the 1970s, a group of Carrboro residents joined together to change the town’s power structure and advocate for a community that fully included all residents. Thanks to their commitment, today Carrboro honors its working-class roots while reaching toward the goals of social equity, environmental harmony, and fiscal responsibility.', 'Carrboro was first settled in 1882 around a University of North Carolina railroad spur. Name: 52_bike_silver_biking_bicyclists
Representative_Docs: ['We are officially in Bike Month in Carrboro with a mix of social rides, a bike breakfast, workshops and other events. \xa0We encourage residents to use bikes for transit and to support bike-friendly infrastructure and policies. \xa0This initiative is directly tied to our climate action goals, aiming to reduce car use and emissions while promoting healthy living. \xa0Here is a short list of events:', 'Did you know? Carrboro became the first community in North Carolina to be designated a Silver Level Bicycle Friendly Community by the League of American Bicyclists.', 'In 2010, Carrboro became the first community in North Carolina to be designated a Silver Level Bicycle Friendly Community by the League of American Bicyclists. Numerous Town plans include policies that support biking, such as the Carrboro Connects Comprehensive Plan, the Updated Bike Plan, and the Safe Routes to School Strategic Action Plan.']

Name: 53_technology_processes_various_effective
Representative_Docs: ['OUR PURPOSE is to assist departments in delivering technology-based solutions that are cost-effective and increase the effectiveness and efficiency of various Town services. This is accomplished in a spirit of partnership and consultation with our stakeholders, which fosters a customer-focused environment that integrates people, processes, and technology. To work with various external and community organizations, as directed by the Town Manager, to discover mutually beneficial relationships sharing technology resources and strengths. Departmental Goals To provide technology that enhances the delivery of Town services and improves access to and the quality of vital government data. To partner with our various customers to understand their business processes and needs and then identify activities that can be effectively streamlined through the application of technology in a manner that is cost-effective and convenient. To continually seek improvement by aligning technology to business processes throughout Town government. Name: 54_weaver_street_study_east
Representative_Docs: ['Home Government Departments Planning, Zoning & Inspections Transportation Transportation Projects Reimagining East Weaver Street Reimagining East Weaver Street Town Council Meetings On Tuesday, February 17, 2026, the Town Council voted to pause the Reimagining Weaver Street Project for at least one year during which time additional engagement be undertaken with downtown businesses, patrons and communities. Watch the Council Meeting Video . On Tuesday, January 20, 2026, the Town Council received a Transportation Update which included information about this project. Watch the Council Meeting Video On Tuesday, October 7, 2025, the Town Council received a presentation and update on the proposal. Agenda materials are posted here Watch the Council Meeting Video . On Tuesday, June 17, 2025, the Town Council received a presentation on this proposal. Watch the Council Meeting Video . Capacity Study The Town engaged an engineering firm to prepare a capacity analysis. NCDOT has completed their review of the study. Copies are provided below East Weaver Street Capacity Study - Sep 2025 East Weaver Street Closure - NCDOT Review Town staff presented the preliminary findings of a draft capacity study to Town Council on June 17, 2025. Agenda materials from the meeting can be found here . A recording of the meeting, where projected impacts of the closure and a demonstration of the traffic model can be viewed below. The study includes the following recommendations: Lane reallocation on East Main Street, and North Greensboro Street to mitigate potential long lines of vehicles waiting at the intersection No change to the loading zone on South Greensboro, because there is not enough pavement width to accommodate an additional travel lane. Maple Avenue is recommended to remain southbound one-way only to accommodate potential congestion. NCDOT recommends the lane restripings-referred to in the study as ‘Build with Improvements’ scenario are completed in tandem with the closure of the corridor. Overview At the January 2025 Work Session , staff was directed to evaluate the possibilities of permanently closing East Weaver Street to vehicles to focus on place-making, enhancing the bike-ped network in downtown, and necessary logistics. \xa0In March 2025, the Town engaged Exult Engineering to study the traffic patterns on and around Weaver Street as part of the development of a capacity analysis. \xa0The scope includes East Main Street, portions of Greensboro Street, Roberson Street, Maple Avenue, and East Braxton Foushee Street to also account for the new parking deck at the Drakeford Library Complex, which is now open. Traffic counts for vehicles, cyclists and pedestrians have been completed, and the consultants anticipate having the analysis finished in May 2025. Planning staff are scheduled to present on the draft capacity study on June 17, 2025 to update the Council on this project and to share the results of the traffic analysis. \xa0Over the summer months, staff also plan to conduct community engagement to get input from businesses, residents, and others who may be impacted by closing East Weaver Street. January 14, 2025, Reimagining Weaver Street Work Session Recording Reimagining Weaver Street Agenda Materials Work Session Presentation The image below, presented to the Town Council in January, shows multimodal counts from the Triangle West Transportation Planning Organization 2023 Mobility Report Card. Cameras Downtown Spring 2025 Community members may have seen temporary counters in the study area over the last couple of weeks. The cameras are used for data collection only; they are not focused enough to capture license plates or faces. They simply serve to count the number of vehicles, bicycles, and pedestrians. Name: 56_waste_solid_collection_works
Representative_Docs: ['We appreciate your patience as we work to return to full service on Solid Waste Collection and all other services provided to the community by Public Works.', 'ℹ️ Solid Waste Collection Carrboro Public Works crews will continue collecting waste throughout the week. One side loader is currently picking up debris, and a second side loader borrowed from Orange County is expected to be in service soon. Regarding dumpster service, one truck borrowed from Chapel Hill is in service collecting dumpsters at commercial properties and apartment complexes. Bulk waste is also being serviced, thanks to a boom truck borrowed from High Point. All efforts are concentrated on areas hardest hit by flood damage. We thank you for your patience.', 'Your patience is appreciated. The fleet shop crew was able to get one side loader back in service and that truck will begin collection of residential solid waste today, beginning with the regular Monday collection route. \xa0 The staff continues to locate resources to fill other solid waste truck fleet needs.']

Name: -1_board_town_sewer_hillsborough
Representative_Docs: ["Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For", "Water and sewer rates fund Hillsborough's water and sewer fund. This enterprise fund pays for providing water and sewer service, including operating, maintaining and improving the water and sewer system. The town's water and sewer rates are reviewed annually as part of the budget process. The rates schedule that is adopted with the budget becomes effective July 1 with the new fiscal year. West Fork Eno Reservoir (Photo by Schnabel Engineering) Board of Commissioners Budget and Finances Financial Services Utilities Department Current Rates In-Town Customers Water Rate $12.39 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $24.78 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $17.44 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $34.88 for up to 2,000 gallons Out-of-Town Customers Water Rate $24.16 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $48.32 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $34.01 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $68.02 for up to 2,000 gallons Proposed Rates In-Town Customers Water Rate $13.32 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $26.64 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $18.75 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $37.50 for up to 2,000 gallons Out-of-Town Customers Water Rate $25.97 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $51.94 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $36.56 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $73.12 for up to 2,000 gallons Each month, water and sewer customers who use the minimum amount charged or less spend a combined: 2.98 cents per gallon in town. 5.82 cents per gallon out of town. Residents used an average of about 3,440 gallons of water and sewer monthly in the last fiscal year —\xa0July 2024 through June 2025. Fees and Charges Schedule About the Rates Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated. Comparing Water and Sewer Rates Comparing rates is not simple, as the cost of service for each water and sewer system is based on a number of diverse factors. Customers should not expect a gallon of water to cost the same across water systems, notes an Environmental Finance Blog article . Annual Rates Research Annual rates research is conducted by the Environmental Finance Center at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. See: North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Dashboard North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Report Characteristics for Hillsborough Characteristics for Hillsborough that affect rates include that it: Is in the Central Pines Council of Government region. Is within the Upper Neuse River watershed and\xa0the Falls Lake watershed. Is affected by wastewater treatment mandates under the Falls Lake Rules, which are state mandates to protect and restore the water quality of Falls Lake. Charges for a minimum usage each billing cycle. Additional use is billed per 1,000 gallons. Charges out-of-town customers a rate that is 1.95 times the rate for in-town customers. Serves about 6,910 accounts and an estimated 13,995 people as of the start of Fiscal Year 2026. Had average residential customer use of about 3,440 gallons of water per month in the last fiscal year — July 2024 through June 2025. Staff Title Phone Email Bradford, Emily Budget Director 919-296-9427 Email"]

Representative_Docs: ["Christmas Trees The town collects Christmas trees with brush collection. Trees\xa0must be: Removed from the stand. Free of tinsel, ornaments, lights and other decorations. Trees also may be brought to any of Orange County’s waste and recycling centers except the center on Bradshaw Quarry Road. Delivery of Bulk Leaves From November through January, public works employees will deliver leaves to those within an 8-mile radius of Hillsborough who would like the leaves for mulch or compost. Priority is given to Hillsborough residents. The minimum amount is half a truckload, or about 10 cubic yards. The delivery area must be accessible by a large truck. To request a delivery, submit a work order or call the administrative support specialist. Special Collections, Fees and Violations Special Collections, Violations and Fees Special collections of leaves or brush outside of normal collection days require a work order and a fee. The minimum fee is $130.\xa0Fees must be paid before collection is scheduled. Submit a work order or call the administrative support specialist. Fees Fees can be paid several ways.\xa0Contact the administrative support specialist for more information. Cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Do not place cash in the deposit box at the back of the building. Credit or debit card through the town's payments and donations website . Violations If yard waste does not comply with requirements, it will not be collected. An employee will leave information on the town's policy for the resident. Videos See our videos with information on managing fallen leaves. Add Native Plants to Your Yard —\xa0Consider managed landscape areas to reduce mowing. See the Sustainability Initiatives page for more information. Hillsborough Loose Leaf Collection —\xa0The town collects loose leaves about once per week November through January. Leave the Leaves —\xa0Consider leaving leaves on your property for benefits to your yard and wildlife.", "Bulk Items The monthly collection occurs the second full week of the month. Sunday is considered the start of the week. See the curbside collections calendar for exact weeks. Bulk items may be placed by the street up to seven calendar days before the scheduled collection. The collection must fit into a pickup truck bed. A fee is assessed for items exceeding the typical load or for collection outside the regular collection week. See the special collections section. Bulk Items Collected Appliances Furniture Grills, if clean and free of charcoal and fuel tanks Mattresses Scrap metal, if clean Unmounted (rimless) tires Water heaters For questions about other items, contact the Public Works Division. Garbage Household garbage collection is provided once a week to single-family homes. Use town-provided carts. Each household is allowed up to two carts. For a\xa0second cart, see the sections on rollout carts and fees. Follow town requirements for when and how carts may be placed. See the section on rollout carts. Place only bagged household garbage in rollout carts. Dispose paint cans with household garbage if they are dried, flattened and lidless. Completely close the cart lid. Remove all rollout carts and uncollected trash from the street by 6 a.m. the day after collection. Garbage Items Not Collected The town does not collect the items below. See Orange County's A to Z Waste and Recycling Guide and Solid Waste Management website for how to recycle or dispose of these items. Batteries Cardboard (must be recycled) Chemical byproducts Construction or demolition materials (includes lumber) Fuel cans or tanks Hazardous waste Medical waste Plastic bottles other than oil bottles (must be recycled) Pressurized containers Radioactive waste Shock sensitive waste Used oil Special Collections Garbage Collection for Residents with Disabilities The town provides free, special garbage collection for residents with disabilities who live alone or have no able-bodied person in the household. Public Works employees retrieve the rollout trash carts from the homes of those who qualify. To qualify,\xa0a doctor must provide written confirmation that the individual cannot move the rollout cart. To continue the service, a doctor’s letter must be provided yearly by May 1. The town will not request personal or health information.\xa0For more information or to apply, contact the administrative support specialist. Special Bulk Items Collection Special collection of bulk items includes: Items that exceed a typical load. Collection outside of the regular collection week. The special collection requires a work order and fee.\xa0The fee begins at $50 and is based on the nature and amount of materials and on the estimated time required to collect them. Fees must be paid before collection is scheduled. Submit a work order or call the administrative support specialist.", "Commercial Waste Independent vendors provide trash collection to multi-family housing communities, businesses, churches and nonprofits. Businesses may use any vendor they choose if town standards are met. See the regulations and an application for a dumpster exemption . Food Waste Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Drop-off Sites Food waste can be dropped off for free at 11 sites in Orange County, including: Eno River Farmers Market in downtown Hillsborough. Orange County 24-hour recycling drop-off site in Hampton Pointe. Orange County waste and recycling center on Walnut Grove Church Road, north of Hillsborough. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality adds public and private composting sites to the NC Food Donation and Compost Resource Map as they become available. Acceptable Items Items that can be dropped off for composting at these sites includes: Food scraps. Food-related paper, including napkins, greasy pizza boxes and staple-free tea bags. Flowers and trimmings from small indoor plants. For more information on the drop-off sites and a comprehensive list of acceptable items, see the Orange County food waste page. Composting at Home Composting can be done at home and can reduce fertilizer, herbicide and water needs for plants. Learn how to get started on the Orange County Cooperative Extension website . For those without the space, conditions or desire for backyard composting, countertop composters can be purchased to provide a soil additive with less effort. Countertop composters may use a combination of grinders and heat to reduce food volume and create compost. Fees and Violations Fees can be paid several ways.\xa0Contact the administrative support specialist for more information. Cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Credit or debit card through the town's payments and donations website . Violations If requirements for collection are not followed, the garbage or bulk items will not be collected. An employee will leave information on the town's policy for the resident. If trash carts and uncollected garbage are not removed from the street as required, a notice of violation will be delivered to the resident by mail or by town staff.\xa0A fee of $25 per day will be assessed if the resident does not comply with the ordinance within five days of the notice date. Recycling and Other Solid Waste Services Orange County collects recycling at curbs on Wednesdays in Hillsborough. See the county's Urban and Town Curbside (Weekly Collection) page for requirements. The county also provides other trash services to Hillsborough residents.\xa0Contact Orange County Solid Waste Management for these services, including: Recycling drop-off sites. Household hazardous waste collection. Construction and demolition waste collection. Solid waste convenience centers. Rollout Carts For collection and safety, carts must be: Placed at the curb between 4 p.m. the day before collection and 6:30 a.m. the day of collection. Placed within 3 feet of the curb, with the handle facing away from the street. Placed at least 4 feet from parked cars, trees or other obstructions. Removed from the street by 6 a.m. the day after collection. See our video Position Your Solid Waste and Recycling Carts Properly . Rollout Cart Delivery and Repair Residents in single-family homes with active utility accounts may receive one free trash rollout cart after account activation if they live in town limits on a publicly owned road. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether a property is in town limits. A fee is charged for a second cart. Payment must be received before delivery is scheduled. See the fees section for how to pay the fee. Place a work order or call the administrative support specialist to request: Cart delivery or repair Second cart"]

Name: 1_backflow_prevention_device_theft
Representative_Docs: ["Customers fund the operation of the town's water and sewer system, including the infrastructure needed to treat, transport and protect water. Please immediately call 911 to report any tampering or theft. See Fees and Charges Schedule for fees and penalties. See Utilities: Prohibited Activities in the Code of Ordinances. Water can be legally and safely obtained from a fire hydrant with an orange box. Billing and Adjustments Start or Stop Service Tampering and Theft of Infrastructure Backflow Prevention Devices The town own some backflow prevention devices on town properties. Most are privately owned. If you see anyone tampering with a backflow prevention device or have experienced a theft of the device: Call 911 to have an officer dispatched. Contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. The devices are housed in utility boxes and prevent contaminated water from entering potable water in a building. They can be targets for theft due to their metal content. Property owners who have had a backflow preventer stolen or damaged will need to employ the services of a plumber. Preventing Theft Options for preventing theft include: Employing community or neighborhood watch prevention, asking community members to report any suspicious behavior to police. Adding a lock to the backflow preventer box or installing a locking box, available from a plumber or from a plumbing warehouse. If locks are added, access must be given to the town. Cutting back any shrubs or other plantings to allow some view of the device and ensuring that an area of at least 5 feet around the utility is free of any growth. Water Meters Any tampering of a meter or service connection will result in the meter being locked and a tampering fee and civil penalty. Service will not be reconnected until all fees are paid in full. Repeated tampering will result in removal of the meter, additional fees to reset and reactivate it, and potential civil or criminal charges. Theft of Water Safety and Operational Concerns Water theft can cause concerns for the public water system including: Water hammer (form of hydraulic shock) in the system that breaks pipes and damages equipment. Lowered water pressure for customers during the time of theft. Contamination of the public water system. Costs associated with repairing damage to the system. Theft from Hydrants If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the utility system superintendent. To help the town prosecute the case: Discreetly take a photo if possible. Note the time, date, location, vehicle name and number, and license plate. Legally Obtained Water Contractors are able to purchase bulk water from the town. They can: Obtain water from the Water Treatment Plant by paying in advance at the Town Hall Annex. Rent water meters to legally obtain water from a fire hydrant. The meters\xa0are housed in an orange box with a backflow prevention device to safely obtain water. See the Start or Stop Service page. Contact Information Adron F. Thompson Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Office Hours — 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Lashley, Joel Utility System Superintendent 919-296-9651 Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email", 'Backflow Prevention Methods Air Gap — An air gap is the vertical space between a water inlet and the rim of a receptacle receiving public water. Examples are a faucet or hose providing water to a bucket, sink or pool.\xa0The air gap must be at least twice the diameter of the water inlet and no smaller than 1 inch. Backflow Assembly — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet and outlet shutoff valves. It has four test cocks, making inline testing possible. Public water systems, which regulate connections to the public water supply, generally require backflow assemblies rather than devices. Backflow Device — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet or outlet shutoff valves and usually does not have test cocks. Inline testing is not possible for devices without test cocks. The devices are not approved by the University of Southern California Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research. Backflow devices generally are governed by plumbing code when a building is constructed or upfitted. Elimination —\xa0Eliminating the cross-connection is necessary when the hazard has been improperly installed and plumbed or when the hazard is no longer in use. Common Backflow Prevention Assemblies and Uses Double Check Valve Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler system with no booster pumps, most commercial establishments Double Check Valve Detector Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as non-health hazards Reduced Pressure Detector Assembly Health and non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as health hazards Reduced Pressure Principal Assembly (preferred assembly) Health and non-health hazards Hospitals, car washes, lawn irrigation, mortuaries Common Backflow Prevention Devices and Uses Pressure Vacuum Breaker Back siphonage only Multi-zone irrigation systems Hose Connection Vacuum Breaker (not testable) Back siphonage only Garden hose connections Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information. Recommendations for Pool and Hot Tub Owners Due to the risk for backflow, the town recommends that customers filling swimming pools, hot tubs, ponds or other bodies of water: Use the air gap method. See the section on backflow prevention methods. Avoid times when hydrants are opened for flushing. This occurs in March and occasionally in the fall. Water System Flushing Lower Water Pressure —\xa0When hydrants are flushed, pressure in the water system drops. Water could backflow into the public water supply system if water main pressure is lower and a hose becomes submerged in a body of water being filled. Chemical Change —\xa0During the annual March flushing, the town’s water is disinfected by chlorination instead of chloramines (ammonia and chlorine). The state requires this change in disinfection for one month each year. The change could upset the balance of a pool, pond or spa.', "The town regulates connections to its water supply to prevent contamination and to protect public safety. Backflow prevention devices ensure water flows only in one direction. A number of uses require installation of a backflow prevention device, including irrigation systems. A backflow prevention device with protection for the winter Tampering and Theft About Backflow What is it? Backflow is the unwanted reversal of the flow of water and substances.\xa0Typically, water will flow from the public water supply to the consumer’s plumbing system. When backflow occurs, the water flows from the non-drinking water source to the drinking water source and potentially contaminates it. What causes it? Backflow incidents are caused by one of the following: Back pressure — This is created when the pressure within the customer’s system becomes greater than the water supply pressure. Elevated tanks, heating systems and booster pumps are some of the main causes of back pressure. Back siphonage — This is created when the water supply main has negative or reduced pressure. Water main breaks or hydrant flushing are two of the main causes of back siphonage. About How the Town Protects Water The town has requirements to protect the public drinking water supply. Containment Protection — The town installs backflow\xa0prevention at the service meter to protect the public water supply from contamination. Isolation Protection — The town requires backflow prevention at the fixture\xa0where water is used. This protects the customer's internal drinking water plumbing from contamination. Uses Requiring Backflow Prevention Several types of facilities are required to install a backflow prevention device. If your property or business type is not included in the list below and you have a specific question about your legal requirements, contact the town's utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Auto repair shops Auxiliary water supply (wells) Barber shops and beauty salons Breweries Buildings that reuse or recycle water Buildings with commercial or public kitchens, groundwater wells, multiple\xa0water service lines, roof tanks and elevated storage lines, or water-cooled equipment or chillers Canneries Commercial car washes Dye plants Exterminators Fire sprinkler system Food preparation facilities Food processing plants and meat or fish packers Funeral parlors Greenhouses Lawn irrigation systems Metal plating, cleaning, processing or fabricating facilities Hospitals, clinics and laboratories (including veterinary hospitals) Ice manufacturing facilities Large residential dwellings with water boilers that use rust-inhibitors or other\xa0water treatment chemicals (treated water boilers) Laundries and dry cleaners Medical and dental offices Morgues, mortuaries and autopsy facilities Nursing homes Paper processors Photo-processing facilities Printing facilities Schools and colleges Sewage treatment plants or handling facilities Slaughterhouses and live poultry processing facilities Swimming pools Supermarkets Tanneries Warehouses with toxic chemical storage Cross-Connection Control Requirements See Cross-Connection Control Policy . No Direct Connections The town prohibits direct connections between the water supply and any potential source of contamination.\xa0Potential sources of contamination must be isolated by an air gap or a reduced pressure zone backflow assembly. A direct connection would occur if a garden hose became submerged in a swimming pool, hot tub, soapy water bucket, pond or pet water dish. Water Use by Businesses Contracted businesses working in Hillsborough and needing water must do one of the following: Transport water into town. Purchase bulk water from the town. Rent a hydrant meter from the town. The orange box that is connected to some hydrants contains a meter and a backflow prevention device. See: Start or Stop Service page for bulk water purchases or hydrant meter rentals Tampering and Theft page Outdoor Faucet Requirements The town requires a hose bib vacuum breaker on every outside faucet. The vacuum breaker must be certified by the NSF 61 standard. The American Water Works Association recommends replacing vacuum breakers every five years or if they are failing. The device has failed if water is spraying out of the breaker's weep holes. Testing The town contracts with Backflow Solutions to manage its backflow test data. Customers using backflow prevention assemblies must: Have the assemblies tested when installed. Maintain their assemblies and h ave them tested annually by qualified individuals. Submit results of testing and maintenance to Backflow Solutions. The company maintains a list of licensed, registered testing agencies on its website. Contact Backflow Solutions at 800-414-4990 or bsionline@backflow.com. Visit its website at BSI Online . Process Existing Assemblies — Licensed testers must submit test results through the Backflow Solutions website . Failed Test\xa0— T he assembly must be repaired within 10 days if it fails a test. A certified tester must submit retest results on the Backflow Solutions website . New Installations — A licensed tester who performed the work must sign a printed test form. A copy must be emailed to Backflow Solutions. Preventing Backflow Backflow Prevention Methods Air Gap — An air gap is the vertical space between a water inlet and the rim of a receptacle receiving public water. Examples are a faucet or hose providing water to a bucket, sink or pool.\xa0The air gap must be at least twice the diameter of the water inlet and no smaller than 1 inch. Backflow Assembly — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet and outlet shutoff valves. It has four test cocks, making inline testing possible. Public water systems, which regulate connections to the public water supply, generally require backflow assemblies rather than devices. Backflow Device — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet or outlet shutoff valves and usually does not have test cocks. Inline testing is not possible for devices without test cocks. The devices are not approved by the University of Southern California Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research. Backflow devices generally are governed by plumbing code when a building is constructed or upfitted.
