These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information. Recommendations for Pool and Hot Tub Owners Due to the risk for backflow, the town recommends that customers filling swimming pools, hot tubs, ponds or other bodies of water: Use the air gap method. See the section on backflow prevention methods. Avoid times when hydrants are opened for flushing. This occurs in March and occasionally in the fall. Water System Flushing Lower Water Pressure —\xa0When hydrants are flushed, pressure in the water system drops. Water could backflow into the public water supply system if water main pressure is lower and a hose becomes submerged in a body of water being filled. Chemical Change —\xa0During the annual March flushing, the town’s water is disinfected by chlorination instead of chloramines (ammonia and chlorine). The state requires this change in disinfection for one month each year. The change could upset the balance of a pool, pond or spa. Contact Information Adron F. Thompson Water/Sewer Facility,\xa0715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email"]

Name: 2_camera_worn_body_car
Representative_Docs: ['Training and Deployment All officers should receive training on the use of the in-car system as part of their field training. Only the department issued camera system may be used. In-car video systems are installed in all marked patrol units. In-car systems are set to activate automatically when the emergency lights are activated or if the vehicle’s speed exceeds 75 mph. Procedures Officers operating a vehicle with an in-car camera are responsible for inspecting the system prior to beginning their tour of duty to ensure it is properly positioned, functioning and that there is adequate storage space. Any problems with in-car camera equipment must be reported to their immediate supervisor, or their commander in their supervisor’s absence. Use of the in-car system’s microphone is optional if an officer is utilizing a body worn camera. In-car cameras should activate automatically as described above. Officers may manually activate the camera if needed. In-car cameras should be activated during any traffic stop; pursuit or emergency response; or any call response or situation where the officer believes the use of the camera advantageous. Officers may deactivate their in-car camera: Upon completion of any traffic stop or citizen contact once the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence. At the end of a pursuit or response, once the officer has arrived and the scene is safe. When the officer has determined that the camera activated due to an automatic trigger and the use of the camera is not required. Once any call or situation that caused the officer to activate the camera has resolved. Exceptions and Restrictions Officers shall not use in-car camera systems to record activities involving known confidential informants or undercover officers unless specifically authorized to do so by a supervisor. In-car camera systems shall not be used to intentionally record conversations between employees without all parties involved being clearly made aware that a recording is being made unless doing so is part of a formal investigation with prior approval from command level personnel. Officers will not utilize an in-car camera system to continuously record free-speech events unless they are actively engaging with people in the event or have been given a specific supervisory directive to do so. Managing Records Officers are responsible for logging into the in-car system at the beginning of their shift and ensuring the date and their information is correct. Officers are responsible for stopping at police headquarters for sufficient time to download videos. If they are not able to do so, or if the files are too large, they should notify their supervisor so arrangements can be made to have the data manually downloaded. No department member may edit, modify, or delete any in-car camera footage outside of normal retention guidelines or without the specific authorization of the police chief.', 'Training and Deployment Prior to being issued or allowed to use a body worn camera, personnel will be provided training on the use of the camera system. Personnel may only use the department provided body worn camera system unless specifically authorized by the chief of police. Body worn camera systems will be available to all officers. When possible, all officers will have a body worn camera individually assigned for their use. Patrol personnel are expected to wear their body worn camera at all times while on duty. Wear of a body worn camera by non-patrol personnel is discretionary on a situational basis. Non-patrol personnel who are covering patrol functions or who reasonably anticipate taking an enforcement action are expected to be wearing a body worn camera. Procedures Personnel assigned or utilizing a BWC are responsible for ensuring the camera is functioning and recording properly prior to and throughout their duty shift. If an employee who is required to use a camera realizes that their camera is non-functional, they must immediately notify their supervisor and secure a replacement for the non-functioning equipment. Patrol personnel assigned a BWC shall activate their camera as follows: When dispatched to any call for service, officers shall activate their BWC as they begin to respond to the call; when taking any self-initiated activity that could be reasonably anticipated to result in an enforcement contact with a community member, the officer shall activate the camera when they begin the self-initiated activity; officers shall activate or reactivate their cameras immediately upon any situation escalating beyond a normal consensual cooperative contact, becoming adversarial in nature or in any situation where a citizen displays hostility, negative views, or dissatisfaction with police services; officers shall activate or reactivate their BWC any time a person in their custody shows any resistance, makes adversarial statements, or displays any bizarre or unusual behaviors; during any consensual search of a person, building, vehicle or other property (the BWC shall be activated prior to asking for the consent to search); and/or, when serving any type of commitment papers and/or providing short distance transportation to citizens. All personnel, regardless of assignment, shall utilize a BWC during: service and execution of a search warrant at a physical location; any warrantless search of buildings, vehicles or other property unless some type of articulable exigency makes it impractical to wait for a camera to arrive on the scene; and/or any situation where the officer sets out with a reasonable expectation that an arrest or enforcement situation may occur, such as attempting to serve warrants. Officers may utilize their BWC in any situation that they feel it is appropriate to do so in order to properly document the events, capture the events for evidentiary or training purposes, or to protect themselves or the department from liability. Officers may deactivate their BWC: when officers have made the initial assessment of a call for service and reasonably believe there is no enforcement action to be taken, have encountered no resistance or adversarial behaviors, and have moved into an investigative phase and are gathering information for reporting purposes or are performing other non-enforcement related activities, such as directing traffic; when any situation in which an enforcement action was or could have been taken has ended and the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence; upon completion of any traffic stop or citizen contact once the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence; and/or, during periods of excessive waiting or processing of a cooperative subject in custody, such as waiting at the magistrate’s office. Officers may deactivate a BWC when inside private property and specifically requested to do so by the property owner or person in control of the property if the situation is non-adversarial in nature and there are multiple officers present. Officers may deactivate the BWC if the situation is non-adversarial in nature and they can specifically articulate that doing so was necessary to keep a situation from escalating or was necessary to gain citizen cooperation. Officers who deactivate a BWC in this situation must notify their supervisor about this deactivation immediately upon completion of the event. Exceptions and Restrictions Officers shall not utilize body worn camera systems in places where there is a reasonable expectation of privacy without some type of articulable exigency or a search warrant. Officers shall not utilize body worn camera systems in restrooms, locker rooms, or other places where they may film others in compromising situations unless they can articulate a specific job-related reason for doing so. Officers shall not use a body worn camera to record activities involving known confidential informants or undercover officers unless specifically authorized to do so by a supervisor. Body worn camera systems shall not be used to intentionally record conversations between employees without all parties involved being clearly made aware that a recording is being made unless doing so is part of a formal investigation with prior approval from command level personnel. Unless there is a specific articulable necessity for doing so, officers should refrain from utilizing their body worn camera inside a medical facility or other location where doing so may conflict with a person’s specific HIPPA medical privacy. Officers will not utilize a body worn camera system to continuously record free-speech events unless they are actively engaging with people in the event or have been given a specific supervisory directive to do so. Managing Records Officers who utilize a body worn camera are responsible for properly tagging or labeling all videos recorded on their device. Officers are responsible for ensuring that the body worn camera is placed for charging and uploading of video at the end of their duty day. If an officer encounters a problem with uploading video, they must notify a supervisor. No personnel may make or retain a copy of any body worn camera video on any personal device, nor may they release any body worn camera recording without command approval. Access to download or otherwise share body worn camera footage is restricted to command personnel. No department member may edit, modify, or delete any body worn camera footage outside of normal retention guidelines or without the specific authorization of the chief of police.', "This policy establishes the guidelines for the Hillsborough Police Department's\xa0use and management of camera systems and for the audio and video recordings produced. Camera systems build and enhance public trust by documenting: Actions of officers. Interactions between officers and community members. The systems are not intended to be used for investigation or to record detailed investigative actions except in limited situations. Use of the camera systems may result in video that is valued as evidence. The ability to collect evidence is secondary to the ability camera systems offer to document actions and interactions. If an officer encounters a problem with uploading video, they must notify a supervisor. Access to download or otherwise share body worn camera footage is restricted to command personnel. No department member may edit, modify, or delete any body worn camera footage outside of normal retention guidelines or without the specific authorization of the chief of police. B. In-Car Camera Systems Training and Deployment All officers should receive training on the use of the in-car system as part of their field training. Only the department issued camera system may be used. In-car video systems are installed in all marked patrol units. In-car systems are set to activate automatically when the emergency lights are activated or if the vehicle’s speed exceeds 75 mph. Procedures Officers operating a vehicle with an in-car camera are responsible for inspecting the system prior to beginning their tour of duty to ensure it is properly positioned, functioning and that there is adequate storage space. Any problems with in-car camera equipment must be reported to their immediate supervisor, or their commander in their supervisor’s absence. Use of the in-car system’s microphone is optional if an officer is utilizing a body worn camera. In-car cameras should activate automatically as described above. Officers may manually activate the camera if needed. In-car cameras should be activated during any traffic stop; pursuit or emergency response; or any call response or situation where the officer believes the use of the camera advantageous. Officers may deactivate their in-car camera: Upon completion of any traffic stop or citizen contact once the citizen is no longer in the officer’s presence. At the end of a pursuit or response, once the officer has arrived and the scene is safe. When the officer has determined that the camera activated due to an automatic trigger and the use of the camera is not required. Once any call or situation that caused the officer to activate the camera has resolved. Exceptions and Restrictions Officers shall not use in-car camera systems to record activities involving known confidential informants or undercover officers unless specifically authorized to do so by a supervisor. In-car camera systems shall not be used to intentionally record conversations between employees without all parties involved being clearly made aware that a recording is being made unless doing so is part of a formal investigation with prior approval from command level personnel. Officers will not utilize an in-car camera system to continuously record free-speech events unless they are actively engaging with people in the event or have been given a specific supervisory directive to do so. Managing Records Officers are responsible for logging into the in-car system at the beginning of their shift and ensuring the date and their information is correct. Officers are responsible for stopping at police headquarters for sufficient time to download videos. If they are not able to do so, or if the files are too large, they should notify their supervisor so arrangements can be made to have the data manually downloaded. No department member may edit, modify, or delete any in-car camera footage outside of normal retention guidelines or without the specific authorization of the police chief. C. Retention and Release Retention and Release All recordings made by departmental camera systems are the property of the Hillsborough Police Department, and are not to be viewed, duplicated, disseminated, destroyed and /or used without legitimate purposes and in accordance with applicable laws and departmental policy. All recordings will be retained in accordance with the N.C. Schedule for Retention, based on the labeling and categorization of each video. Video may be retained longer due to manual management or department needs. Overall management of departmental video systems, to include body worn camera, In-car and facility systems will be the responsibility of the administrative lieutenant or a designee of the chief of police. The release (providing copies) or disclosure (allow viewing) of body worn camera or in-car recordings will be done in accordance with North Carolina General Statute 132-1.4A, which states that recordings are neither a public record nor are they a personnel record. Any release and disclosure of body worn camera or in-car recordings must be approved by the chief of police. Requests for disclosure of video must be made in writing to the chief of police, and must include the date and approximate time of the activity, or otherwise identify the activity with reasonable particularity sufficient to identify the recording being requested, and/or, Release of videos generally will be done pursuant to a superior court order. Any portion of an audio/video that records events surrounding a violation of the laws prosecutable in the criminal courts of this state or the United States, may be considered a record of a criminal investigation, as described in NCGS 132-1.4. Any portion of an audio/video that is used to subject an employee to departmental disciplinary action may become part of that employee’s personnel file, as defined in NCGS 160A-168, and is open to inspection only as provided by statute. D. Video Requests for Official Use Video Requests for Official Use It is the responsibility of the investigating officer in any case to request that video be retained. This includes body worn camera, in-car and video from department facility systems such as the DWI room. Requests for video will be made using the Video Request Form (form 406b). This form is to be completed and submitted to the administrative lieutenant within two days of the event or the officer becoming aware of the need to retain video. E. Supervisor Responsibilities and Auditing Supervisor Responsibilities and Auditing Supervisors are responsible for ensuring that those under their supervision are properly equipped and utilizing any assigned camera system as directed by this policy. Supervisors are responsible for immediately addressing any deficiency or non-operational camera system that they become aware of, including securing replacement equipment when possible and notifying command personnel and specifically describing the problem. Supervisors and command personnel are expected to randomly audit video footage as directed to ensure policy compliance and to look for training issues. The focus of the random auditing is to be corrective rather than punitive in nature, however significant policy violations will be addressed. Random auditing will be documented using the Video Review Form (form 406a). At least annually, a body worn camera audit will be conducted in which calls for service and body worn camera footage are compared over a multi-day period for each patrol squad to ensure policy compliance."]

Name: 3_stormwater_runoff_geese_pollution
Representative_Docs: ["Rights and Reasonable Use In North Carolina, common law governs stormwater runoff and drainage. Basically, a downhill property has to receive water from uphill. Water has a right to go where it wants to go ― downhill. Components of this common law: The easement of an uphill owner includes the right to accelerate and increase the natural flow of water. Natural flow means the uphill owner cannot divert or cause water to flow onto the lower land in a different manner or place than it would naturally. The downhill owner may not obstruct natural flow from above. Property owners have the legal right to make reasonable use of their property, even if development alters water flow and affects downstream owners. The uphill owner can incur liability when the change in runoff is unreasonable and causes substantial damage. Documents Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Maintenance Responsibilities Property owners are responsible for the stormwater control measures on their private property. Owners include homeowners associations. Drainage Ditches Along Streets Property owners are required to mow drainage ditches along town streets. The town ensures water flow is not restricted in the drainage ditches along town streets. Contact the Public Works Division to request maintenance of drainage ditches. Wet and Dry Ponds Property owners are required to have  wet and dry ponds inspected annually by a private licensed inspector. The inspection report must be submitted to the Stormwater and Environmental Services Division. The third-party annual inspections may result in recommendations for maintenance or improvements. Before making a costly repair, contact town staff to check that the work is necessary. See the Ponds and Geese section if a recommendation involves addressing geese. Ponds and Geese The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division requires a 6-foot vegetated area around ponds used as stormwater control measures. In this area, non-woody vegetation must be allowed to grow to a natural height during the growing season. When this area is newly planted, Canada geese may eat or pull out the vegetation before it is fully established. Goose management can be used to ensure this vegetation is established and maintained. Once grown to a natural height, this vegetation can also help deter geese from accessing the pond. This deterrence method works best if the vegetated area is maintained consistently around the entire pond. Canada geese are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act . They are attracted to ponds, including those used as stormwater control measures. Several geese living by a pond can hurt water quality. They eat young grass shoots that help keep pollution from reaching a pond, and they drop fecal matter that can run into the water. Lack of vegetation can cause shoreline erosion, and both erosion and excessive fecal matter can lead to odor problems, algal blooms and fish kills in the pond and downstream of the pond. To improve water quality, pond management companies may suggest ways to manage Canada geese. The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division does not require property owners to deter or chase away geese. Deterring Geese Hazing Canada geese is a legal option in North Carolina for deterring geese. See more information about hazing on the North Carolina Wildlife Resources Commission's website. Some additional methods for deterring geese include planting tall vegetation and installing temporary or permanent fencing. Often, pond owners with the most success are those who hire a management company to help them and who use multiple methods simultaneously to deter geese. Stream Identification and Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot buffer of plantings along streams and the Eno River. Prior approval is required to clear, build or make other impacts within a stream or river buffer, also called a riparian buffer. See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for additional information and to request stream identification services. See Riparian (Stream) Buffers . About Stream and River Buffers Buffers along water bodies are undisturbed, naturally forested areas that: Provide wildlife food and habitat. Slow stormwater runoff by allowing it to soak into the ground. Buffers help protect water quality by: Controlling erosion and keeping stream banks from collapsing. Reducing flooding and pollution of waterways. Moderating stream temperature through the amount and quality of sunlight allowed on a waterbody. Woody debris in waterways also protect water quality and provide habitat for aquatic organisms. See the document Woody Debris in Streams and Rivers . Resources The division offers materials to help property owners manage stormwater and protect the environment. Contact staff to request a site visit and advice. Documents Compost Blankets Designing A Rain Garden Don't Go Down the Drain — effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses Lawn Care For Water Quality Stormwater Bioswales Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Woody Debris In Streams And Rivers Websites These sites were created in partnership with\xa0Elon University students: Enhance Your Yard with Green Infrastructure — Focusing on water management that works with the natural water cycle. Climate Impact in Residential Areas — Spotlighting the effects of climate change on the local area and actions community members can take. Videos Rain Garden", "The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division reduces stormwater runoff pollution from reaching local water bodies by: Complying with the town’s National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System permit. Regulating development practices. Educating the public about ways to reduce stormwater runoff impacts. Stormwater staff frequently make site visits to assess stormwater runoff issues in the community. Rain Barrel Program Stormwater Social Media Stormwater Fees Town Stormwater Maintenance Stormwater Permits Report Illegal Dumping into Stormwater System About Stormwater Stormwater is rainfall or snow melt that flows across surfaces into waterways. It is North Carolina’s top water-quality problem. Stormwater is not treated and often picks up pollutants as it moves across surfaces, like exposed soil, pavement and rooftops. Pollutants include: Chemicals, oil and grease Excess fertilizer and pet waste Sediment and trash The pollution can harm aquatic ecosystems and drinking water supplies. Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Documents Climate Impact — Rainfall intensity's effect on flooding Do You Like to Fish — Stormwater runoff pollution and its effect on fish State and Federal Regulations Hillsborough must adhere to stormwater runoff regulations from the federal and state governments. Federal Regulation Stormwater is regulated under the 1972 Federal Water Pollution Control Act, commonly called the Clean Water Act. The Environmental Protection Agency regulates discharges through the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System . Discharges include stormwater. State Regulation Within the state, the N.C. Department of Environmental Quality operates the\xa0stormwater program for the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System. Under Phase II of the rules, small municipalities that own and operate separate storm sewers must obtain a permit and  reduce stormwater pollutants from entering their systems through six minimum measures. The minimum measures for Hillsborough are: Public education and outreach on stormwater impacts. Public involvement and participation. Illicit discharge detection and elimination. Construction site stormwater runoff control (erosion control). Post-construction stormwater management for new development and redevelopment. Pollution prevention and good housekeeping for municipal operations. Local Regulations Hillsborough is required to regulate runoff within town limits. See the effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses in the document Don't Go Down the Drain . See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for: Construction and post-construction stormwater management requirements and applications. Information on riparian (stream) buffers and stream identification requests. Town Stormwater Plans Bellevue Branch Watershed Improvement Plan Stormwater Management Plan Illegal Dumping and Connections Report any suspected stormwater pollution or illegal dumping into the town's stormwater system. It is illegal to: Place anything other than stormwater into any part of the town’s stormwater system, including storm drains and ditches. Connect pipes to the town’s stormwater system without approval. Illegal Dumping Signs of illegal dumping: Excessive algae Floating debris Oil sheen Strong pungent or musty odor Storm pipes flowing in dry weather Surface scum or foam Unusual color or cloudiness Examples of illegal dumping: Chlorinated backwash or the draining of swimming pools Contaminated foundation drains Oil, antifreeze, chemicals, garbage, paint or cleaning fluids Sewer and septic tank discharges Untreated animal waste Wash waters from commercial and industrial activities Washing machine discharges Required Detection The town is required to find and eliminate stormwater pollution. If an illegal connection or discharge is found, the property owner will be notified. The owner could face civil penalties up to $5,000 if the issue is not corrected. News Property Owners Recognized for Outstanding Stormwater Maintenance June 23, 2026 8:00 a.m. More News The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division reduces stormwater runoff pollution from reaching local water bodies by: Complying with the town’s National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System permit. Regulating development practices. Educating the public about ways to reduce stormwater runoff impacts. Stormwater staff frequently make site visits to assess stormwater runoff issues in the community. Rain Barrel Program Stormwater Social Media Stormwater Fees Town Stormwater Maintenance Stormwater Permits Report Illegal Dumping into Stormwater System About Stormwater Stormwater is rainfall or snow melt that flows across surfaces into waterways. It is North Carolina’s top water-quality problem. Stormwater is not treated and often picks up pollutants as it moves across surfaces, like exposed soil, pavement and rooftops. Pollutants include: Chemicals, oil and grease Excess fertilizer and pet waste Sediment and trash The pollution can harm aquatic ecosystems and drinking water supplies. Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Documents Climate Impact — Rainfall intensity's effect on flooding Do You Like to Fish — Stormwater runoff pollution and its effect on fish State and Federal Regulations Hillsborough must adhere to stormwater runoff regulations from the federal and state governments. Federal Regulation Stormwater is regulated under the 1972 Federal Water Pollution Control Act, commonly called the Clean Water Act. The Environmental Protection Agency regulates discharges through the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System . Discharges include stormwater. State Regulation Within the state, the N.C. Department of Environmental Quality operates the\xa0stormwater program for the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System. Under Phase II of the rules, small municipalities that own and operate separate storm sewers must obtain a permit and  reduce stormwater pollutants from entering their systems through six minimum measures. The minimum measures for Hillsborough are: Public education and outreach on stormwater impacts. Public involvement and participation. Illicit discharge detection and elimination. Construction site stormwater runoff control (erosion control). Post-construction stormwater management for new development and redevelopment. Pollution prevention and good housekeeping for municipal operations. Local Regulations Hillsborough is required to regulate runoff within town limits. See the effect of stormwater runoff pollution on businesses in the document Don't Go Down the Drain . See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for: Construction and post-construction stormwater management requirements and applications. Information on riparian (stream) buffers and stream identification requests. Town Stormwater Plans Bellevue Branch Watershed Improvement Plan Stormwater Management Plan Illegal Dumping and Connections Report any suspected stormwater pollution or illegal dumping into the town's stormwater system. It is illegal to: Place anything other than stormwater into any part of the town’s stormwater system, including storm drains and ditches. Connect pipes to the town’s stormwater system without approval. Illegal Dumping Signs of illegal dumping: Excessive algae Floating debris Oil sheen Strong pungent or musty odor Storm pipes flowing in dry weather Surface scum or foam Unusual color or cloudiness Examples of illegal dumping: Chlorinated backwash or the draining of swimming pools Contaminated foundation drains Oil, antifreeze, chemicals, garbage, paint or cleaning fluids Sewer and septic tank discharges Untreated animal waste Wash waters from commercial and industrial activities Washing machine discharges Required Detection The town is required to find and eliminate stormwater pollution. If an illegal connection or discharge is found, the property owner will be notified. The owner could face civil penalties up to $5,000 if the issue is not corrected. Stormwater and Your Property Rights and Reasonable Use In North Carolina, common law governs stormwater runoff and drainage. Basically, a downhill property has to receive water from uphill. Water has a right to go where it wants to go ― downhill. Components of this common law: The easement of an uphill owner includes the right to accelerate and increase the natural flow of water. Natural flow means the uphill owner cannot divert or cause water to flow onto the lower land in a different manner or place than it would naturally. The downhill owner may not obstruct natural flow from above. Property owners have the legal right to make reasonable use of their property, even if development alters water flow and affects downstream owners. The uphill owner can incur liability when the change in runoff is unreasonable and causes substantial damage. Documents Stormwater Runoff Conveyances Water Flows Downhill Maintenance Responsibilities Property owners are responsible for the stormwater control measures on their private property. Owners include homeowners associations. Drainage Ditches Along Streets Property owners are required to mow drainage ditches along town streets. The town ensures water flow is not restricted in the drainage ditches along town streets. Contact the Public Works Division to request maintenance of drainage ditches. Wet and Dry Ponds Property owners are required to have  wet and dry ponds inspected annually by a private licensed inspector. The inspection report must be submitted to the Stormwater and Environmental Services Division. The third-party annual inspections may result in recommendations for maintenance or improvements. Before making a costly repair, contact town staff to check that the work is necessary. See the Ponds and Geese section if a recommendation involves addressing geese. Ponds and Geese The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division requires a 6-foot vegetated area around ponds used as stormwater control measures. In this area, non-woody vegetation must be allowed to grow to a natural height during the growing season. When this area is newly planted, Canada geese may eat or pull out the vegetation before it is fully established. Goose management can be used to ensure this vegetation is established and maintained. Once grown to a natural height, this vegetation can also help deter geese from accessing the pond. This deterrence method works best if the vegetated area is maintained consistently around the entire pond. Canada geese are protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act . They are attracted to ponds, including those used as stormwater control measures. Several geese living by a pond can hurt water quality. They eat young grass shoots that help keep pollution from reaching a pond, and they drop fecal matter that can run into the water. Lack of vegetation can cause shoreline erosion, and both erosion and excessive fecal matter can lead to odor problems, algal blooms and fish kills in the pond and downstream of the pond. To improve water quality, pond management companies may suggest ways to manage Canada geese. The Stormwater and Environmental Services Division does not require property owners to deter or chase away geese. Deterring Geese Hazing Canada geese is a legal option in North Carolina for deterring geese. See more information about hazing on the North Carolina Wildlife Resources Commission's website. Some additional methods for deterring geese include planting tall vegetation and installing temporary or permanent fencing. Often, pond owners with the most success are those who hire a management company to help them and who use multiple methods simultaneously to deter geese. Stream Identification and Riparian Buffers The town enforces a 50-foot buffer of plantings along streams and the Eno River. Prior approval is required to clear, build or make other impacts within a stream or river buffer, also called a riparian buffer. See the Public Infrastructure Permits page for additional information and to request stream identification services. See Riparian (Stream) Buffers . About Stream and River Buffers Buffers along water bodies are undisturbed, naturally forested areas that: Provide wildlife food and habitat. Slow stormwater runoff by allowing it to soak into the ground. Buffers help protect water quality by: Controlling erosion and keeping stream banks from collapsing. Reducing flooding and pollution of waterways. Moderating stream temperature through the amount and quality of sunlight allowed on a waterbody. Woody debris in waterways also protect water quality and provide habitat for aquatic organisms. See the document Woody Debris in Streams and Rivers . Resources The division offers materials to help property owners manage stormwater and protect the environment. Contact staff to request a site visit and advice. Name: 4_tourism_tax_food_prepared
Representative_Docs: ['Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Rate The town levies a 1% sales tax on prepared foods and beverages. Proceeds fund the Tourism Board . The volunteer board\xa0reinvests the funds into: Hillsborough Visitors Center Grants, special projects and partnerships Contract services with local organizations and historic sites See Prepared Food and Beverage Tax in the Code of Ordinances. Monthly Tax Compliance Monthly reports and tax payments for prepared food and beverage are: Due by the 25th day of the month after sales were made. Subject to penalties for nonpayment, including a possible misdemeanor charge punishable by a fine and imprisonment. Required every month from permanent mobile food vendors regardless of sales. This is in instead of paying a single-day prepaid food and beverage tax for each day of operation. Payment Methods Online with credit or debit card using the town payments and donations website. In person with cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hours are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Forms Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Report Form Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Report Form (Español) Single-Day Tax Compliance Nonprofit organizations and merchants selling prepared food or beverages in temporary locations must submit an application and pay for a single-day prepaid food and beverage tax for each day of operation. Temporary locations include food trucks. Multiple dates may be listed on the same permit application. A separate $15 pre-paid tax must be paid for each day of operation. The tax is levied on the individual consumer (not the merchant or seller). Sales are exempt only if the end-user is tax exempt under an appropriate provision of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code. Merchants selling in temporary locations: Are subject to penalties for nonpayment of the tax, including a possible misdemeanor charge punishable by a fine and imprisonment. Are also referred to as itinerant merchants. See Prepared Food and Beverage Tax in\xa0the Code of Ordinances. Payment Methods Online using the town payments and donations website. In person with cash or check at the Financial Services Department in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hours are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Forms Single-Day Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Application Single-Day Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Application (Español)', "Each year, the Hillsborough Board of Commissioners considers funding partnership opportunities with nonprofit agencies to fulfill an identified town need or\xa0advance the town’s strategic objectives and priorities. Additionally, nonprofit agencies may seek funding from the two volunteer boards charged with overseeing the town's prepared food and beverage tax and its room occupancy tax. Town of Hillsborough Funding Through nonprofit partnerships, the town provides funding for a limited time to qualified agencies that offer services the town can legally fund. This program is a part of the annual budget cycle. Application Period: Jan. 9-March 31 Funding Policy Read-only Application Apply for Nonprofit Partnership Funds Tourism Funding About Tourism Funding The two Hillsborough appointed boards that oversee proceeds from taxes also reinvest the funds into tourism. Tourism Board — Reinvests funds from a 1% sales tax on prepared foods and beverages\xa0into: Hillsborough Visitors Center Grants, special projects and partnerships Contract services with local organizations and historic sites Tourism Development Authority — Reinvests funds from a 3% hotel and motel room occupancy tax\xa0to encourage visitors to stay in local lodging facilities. Special Projects and Partnerships Both the Tourism Board and Tourism Development Authority provide funds for special projects and partnerships. The projects and partnerships are ge nerally small in scope and ne eded only once. Requests must demonstrate or explain how the project or partnership will: Expand tourism if funded by either board. Increase Hillsborough lodging occupancies if funded by\xa0the Tourism Development Authority. During its March-June grant cycle, the Tourism Board generally does not accept requests for special projects and partnerships. Contact Staff Support Tourism Board Grants The Tourism Board accepts grant applications for a wide range of special events and projects designed to increase tourism to Hillsborough. February-March — The application period is open. April — Applications are competitively scored. May —\xa0Awards are announced at the Tourism Board meeting. June —\xa0Applicants receive official notification of funding status. The board requires a final report and the return of unused grant funds after a project has concluded. Name: 5_park_parks_recreation_gold
Representative_Docs: ["Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. These recreation facilities require cleanup after a severe weather event to remove hazards. They are open for use many more days throughout the year than they are closed for weather and maintenance. Built to Withstand Flooding Riverwalk is located partially within Gold Park and was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Gold Park’s dog park was built with a chain-link fence to allow floodwaters through. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence. Severe Weather Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans.", "Hillsborough offers many opportunities for recreation. It does not provide recreational programming. Town-maintained parks and greenways are open daily from 7 a.m. until 30 minutes after sunset. The Public Space and Sustainability Division: Manages town parks and greenways. Implements decisions of the Parks and Recreation Board . Gold Park includes swings and other play equipment. Parks and Recreation Board Proposed Greenway Reservations Riverwalk Greenway Town Map Town Parks and Greenways See the Town Map for directions and amenities. Greenways Parks Calvin Street Greenway — This short greenway\xa0allows pedestrian and bicycle access from downtown and adjacent neighborhoods to Gold Park and Riverwalk. Riverwalk Greenway —\xa0This greenway along the Eno River connects Occoneechee Mountain State Natural Area and trails east of town. See the Riverwalk page. Community Parks Cates Creek Park —\xa01445 Cates Creek Parkway. This community park has a pollinator garden and a skate spot . The multi-use field with soccer goals can be reserved. See the Reservations and Bee City USA pages. Gold Park —\xa0415 Dimmocks Mill Road. This community park includes a dog park and a part of the Riverwalk greenway. The multi-use field and large picnic shelter can be reserved. See the Reservations and Bee City USA pages. See the park's trail map . Natural Area Kings Highway Park —\xa01001 Ben Johnston Road. This natural area with trails is surrounded in part by Lake Ben Johnston. It includes a kayak launch . Neighborhood and Pocket Parks Hillsborough Heights Park —\xa0510 Terrell Road Mayo Park — South side of West King Street Murray Street Park —\xa0225 Murray St. Turnip Patch Park —\xa0333 W. King St. Dog Park The dog park is a fenced-in area in Gold Park where dogs area allowed to be off leash. It has the same hours as Gold Park. Dog Park Rules Dogs must be: At least 6 months old and not in heat. Current on all required vaccinations, with rabies tags displayed. On a maximum 6-foot leash before entering and when leaving the facility. Within sight and under voice control of owner at all times. Immediately removed if showing aggression. Owners must: Collect and dispose of all dog waste. Help keep the off-leash area safe for all visitors. Report all dog bites to Orange County Animal Control. Call 919-942-7387 or 911. Ground Covering The dog park's ground is covered with shredded hardwood mulch that: Is safe for use by children and dogs. Helps reduce dust, dirt and the ground cover’s movement. Grass has not been planted because maintenance would be difficult with the park's heavy use and because establishing grass would require several seasons of nonuse. Self-Guided Walks Interpretive Signage The town's interpretive signage helps provide context to sites within Hillsborough. The signs provide understanding of the town's history, environment and culture. Most of the signs are along the Riverwalk greenway, but there are some at other key points of interest in town. See Signage Map Treasure Trees The Treasure Trees Program recognizes the preservation of significant trees in the Hillsborough area. Trees are selected by the Tree Board. View them on the Treasure Trees page. About Our Recreation No recreation is allowed at the town's reservoir. See the About Water page. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans. Other Recreation Recreation Offerings from Others Exchange Club Park is owned and maintained by the Exchange Club. Contact the club at 919-732-9283 for more information. Orange County provides recreational programming, a senior center and additional parks in and around town, including Fairview and River parks. See the county's: Trails and greenways map Parks locator map For other recreation options in the area and maps, visit the Hillsborough Visitors Center . Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9600 Office Hours —\xa08 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans. Other Recreation Recreation Offerings from Others Exchange Club Park is owned and maintained by the Exchange Club. Contact the club at 919-732-9283 for more information. Orange County provides recreational programming, a senior center and additional parks in and around town, including Fairview and River parks. See the county's: Trails and greenways map Parks locator map For other recreation options in the area and maps, visit the Hillsborough Visitors Center . Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9600 Office Hours —\xa08 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Public Space and Sustainability Staff Title Phone Email Rhew, Lindsay Public Works and Public Space Administrative Support Specialist 919-296-9600 Email Trueblood, Stephanie Public Space and Sustainability Manager 919-296-9481 Email"]
