Name: 7_training_law_basic_enforcement
Representative_Docs: ["About the Training Becoming a police officer in North Carolina requires successfully completing a basic law enforcement training program.\xa0The full-time program takes about 16-20 weeks to complete and concludes with a comprehensive written exam and skills testing. The program\xa0prepares entry-level individuals with the cognitive and physical skills needed to become certified law enforcement officers. It includes: 36 blocks of instruction with topics including: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law Practical exercises Extensive ethics training woven throughout the course. Minimum requirements to attend the basic training include being: A United States citizen. At least 20 years old. A high school graduate or having passed the General Educational Development tests. Being of good moral character. Basic law enforcement training programs are offered through many community colleges across the state. More information is available on the North Carolina Justice Academy website . Basic Training Sponsorship Individuals wishing to attended a BLET program can request that the Hillsborough Police Department sponsor them. Sponsorship allows the program’s $900 community college tuition to be waived. The individual is responsible for costs associated with books, uniforms and other supplies. Sponsorship is not an offer of employment, and neither the individual nor the agency is making a commitment. Those wishing to be considered for sponsorship must complete an application online through the town's employment website . All required documents must be submitted, including the F-3 personal history statement and the liability release. Those seeking to be considered for sponsorship must indicate this on the supplemental question that is part of the application. Sponsorship requests are considered on a case-by-case basis and generally do not involve a specific process and only minor background checks.", 'The Police Department prioritizes developing and maintaining skills. It requires training beyond the\xa0minimum number of hours and topics required by the state each year. A number of Hillsborough officers are trained to provide instruction in house and to officers in other agencies. New Officers Basic Law Enforcement Training Entry-level police officers must complete basic law enforcement training. The  course contains 640 hours of instruction and practical exercises and takes about 16 weeks to complete. It contains 36 blocks of instruction on such topics as: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law An extensive ethics section is woven throughout the training experience. Field Training Newly hired officers are assigned a field training officer and receive field training on: Community service Day-to-day activities of a police officer Practical aspects of law enforcement During field training, new officers ride with the field training officer, an experienced officer who is trained to teach, coach and evaluate the new officer. The new officer must meet performance standards and pass an examination provided orally through a panel interview. Annual Training Canine Training Police dogs are certified annually by an independent trainer. Each month, the canine officers and their handlers train for at least 16 hours in a variety of scenarios and in different locations and weather conditions. They also train with handlers and dogs from surrounding agencies. Elective Training Officers have an annual minimum goal of 40 hours of elective training — external training that they can choose to attend beyond their required training. Hillsborough officers are allowed up to 120 hours of elective training per year. Mandatory In-Service Training Certified officers are required by the state to receive 24 hours of annual in-service training. The state requires training in: Domestic violence Ethics Firearms Juvenile minority sensitivity Legal updates Proactive wellness Officers may choose from other topics to complete their required training, including: Engaging with people with views more extreme than the majority Enhancing community engagement Learning from local leaders who have dealt with civil unrest Partnering with community professionals Reducing the intensity of situations through listening, talking and defensive techniques Driver Training Slower Is Faster Driver Training The town provides annual driver training to its officers. In addition, the town manager and police chief help provide driver training to other police departments in the state through a course modeled after the Hillsborough "Slower Is Faster" program and sponsored by the\xa0N.C. League of Municipalities Risk Management Services Division. Claims from police vehicle collisions are one of the largest liabilities and costs that the league\'s insurance pool must pay for each year. Vehicle-related accidents also are the leading cause of police line-of-duty deaths and cause many deaths within communities. Tactics to Reduce Collisions Town Manager Eric Peterson competes in motorsports and coordinates the annual training in Hillsborough, which emphasizes key tactics to perform while driving to reduce collisions: Looking ahead and scanning surroundings to avoid surprises. Braking early. Slowing down to leave a margin for error for the unexpected. Using self-talk to better perform under pressure. Breathing to remain calm. The tactics help officers to keep their vehicles and cognitive functions under control. Adrenaline can impair driving by causing tunnel vision, loss of hearing, slowed reaction time, decreased motor skills, and a slowing of forebrain functions and cognitive processing. Training includes classroom instruction and practice behind the wheel that is focused on improving decision-making through various scenarios, including the sudden appearance of objects in the roadway. An instructor rides along to provide coaching. Internal and External Training Options Internal Training The Police Department offers a variety of training in house, including the following. Jiu Jitsu Training The Police Department provides training in Brazilian jiu jitsu to increase officers\' confidence in their ability to safely bring calm to intense and dangerous situations. The intent  is to reduce the need for use of force. Jiu jitsu is a martial arts grappling technique that emphasizes taking an opponent to the ground, gaining a dominant position and using techniques to safely force the person into submission. The techniques allow dominance from any position and negate size and strength advantages. They allow officers to safely take someone into custody, including: Safely handcuffing a person. Safely stopping a person from hitting or hurting an officer. Safely extracting a person\'s arms if the person is face down possibly with a firearm or a weapon underneath. Virtual Training The Police Department owns a virtual reality training system that allows up to two officers to train together in a small space with a controlled environment.\xa0The training helps officers: Fine-tune work done on a daily basis, like vehicle stops. Learn how to safely respond to a variety of situations. Review where improvements are needed in their response. Officers put on a headset and trackers for their wrist and tools. They train in scenarios created by the department or provided through the system, such as an active shooter situation. Officers are told what their scenario will be and the expected outcome. Their responses are unscripted. A trainer monitors the training on a screen, where avatars for the training officers can be seen.\xa0A body camera view allows the trainer to see where the officer’s eyes are. The virtual training can: Be manipulated to interact with the officers and their responses. Provide training for more real-life situations than basic training can offer. External Training Hillsborough officers have achieved certification in a number of trainings offered externally. Crisis Intervention Crisis intervention training creates connections among law enforcement, mental health providers, hospital emergency services and individuals with mental illness and their families. The training improves communication, identifies mental health resources for those in crisis, and ensures officer and community safety. Law Enforcement Executive Program The Law Enforcement Executive Program at N.C. State University provides twenty days of training over six months to effectively manage\xa0critical challenges. Participants enter the program with\xa0good basic managerial skills and a strong working knowledge of North Carolina local government and police service. Through the training, they build on those foundations and: Explore applying public management ideas and techniques for police agencies. Sharpen personal skills to excel as police managers. Think creatively about law enforcement management in increasingly complex, uncertain and changing environments. Professional Certification Officers can earn intermediate and advanced law enforcement certificates through the\xa0Law Enforcement Officers’ Professional Certificate Program. The program was established by the Criminal Justice Education and Training Standards Commission to: Foster interest in college education and professional law enforcement training Attract highly qualified people into law enforcement West Point Leadership Training The West Point Leadership Course at Methodist University in Fayetteville is adapted from the military leadership course that is required of all cadets in their third year of study at the United States Military Academy at West Point. The academically rigorous course is adapted for use in non-military organizations and relies heavily on case-based learning. The training examines and integrates leadership from the perspective of the individual, group, leader and organization. Contact Information Police Station, 127 N. Churton St. 919-296-9500 919-732-2187 (fax) Office phones are answered 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Call 911 for emergencies or to have an officer dispatched. Contact Police Department Staff Title Phone Email Hooks, Richard Captain 919-296-9524 Email Chelenza, Nicholas Lieutenant 919-296-9523 Email', "The Hillsborough Police Department is always looking for individuals to join our team who have a high moral character and a genuine desire to serve and make a difference. The Town of Hillsborough is an equal opportunity employer that welcomes applicants from diverse backgrounds. All applicants will be treated in a fair and consistent manner. Selection for employment will be made based on the applicants' qualifications for the job. Opportunities may be available for: Individuals with no experience who are interested in law enforcement. Individuals looking to start a career who have completed basic law enforcement training. Lateral transfers from other agencies. Apply Police Information Recruitment Video Benefits Benefits of Joining HPD The Town of Hillsborough offers a competitive benefits package, including: Salary — The starting salary for an officer who has completed basic law enforcement training is $58,966. Hiring incentives also may be offered: 2% increase for an associate’s degree or 4% for a bachelor’s degree in a related field. Up to 3% increase for each year of comparable service, up to 25%. $1,500 for Spanish fluency. Shift differential of $4 per hour between midnight and 7 a.m. Officers may attain up to two specializations with 2.5% pay increase for each. Specializations include: Canine unit Collision investigation specialist Community engagement specialist Investigative specialist Tactical and medical specialist Traffic and drug enforcement specialist Pay scales are as follows: $58,966 to $94,345 for police officer $61,739 to $98,782 for police officer 1st class $64,679 to $103,487 for corporal Career progression — Hillsborough's two-step career progression system allows officers to advance their careers outside of regular performance evaluations and\xa0opportunities for promotion. Officers who complete certain training requirements can be eligible at 12 months of service for officer 1st class, the first step of the career progression. Each step up to corporal provides a 5% salary increase. Individually assigned vehicles — Each officer is assigned a vehicle. Officers living within 30 miles of Hillsborough may be eligible to take the vehicle home. Tuition assistance — The department prioritizes education. The town will provide up to $5,250 in tuition reimbursement each year. Training — The department also prioritizes developing and maintaining skills. Officers have an annual minimum goal of 40 hours of elective training (external training that officers choose to attend beyond their required training). Officers are allowed up to 120 hours of elective training per year. Workout time — Officers may work out for up to 1.5 hours on duty. Ask about other physical fitness incentives. Disqualifiers Disqualifiers Factors that will disqualify a candidate from joining the department include: Criminal history — Any felony or crime conviction that could have required more than two years of incarceration as punishment. Conviction of a Class B misdemeanor within the preceding five years. Conviction of multiple misdemeanor, non-traffic crimes within the preceding five years. Any history of domestic violence. A history of criminal charges that reflect poorly on the candidate’s character. Traffic violations — Having a driver's license suspended or revoked after the date of application or\xa0in the three years before. Any history of driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs. Multiple at-fault accidents or driving violations beyond minor speeding or right-of-way violations. Drug use —\xa0Illegal drug use beyond youthful experimentation. The department evaluates prior drug use for its extent and for how recent it was. Credit —\xa0Demonstrated history of financial irresponsibility. Other — Former military personnel discharged dishonorably and ineligible for reenlistment. Untruthfulness or intentional withholding of information from any application, interview or paperwork associated with the position. Deliberate inaccuracies or incomplete statements. Cheating on any examination or testing associated with the position. Actions or activities that call into question the candidate’s character, integrity or morality. Hiring Process Lateral Transfers and New Hire Information Police Officer candidates and non-sworn police department applicants must meet the minimum standards as required by the North Carolina Criminal Justice Standards Commission. Police Officer applicants must have completed Basic Law Enforcement Training (BLET) and be eligible for sworn status in the State of North Carolina before assuming the role of officer. Successful candidates must adhere to standards set forth in sub chapter 09B of the North Carolina Criminal Justice Employment: Education & Training. The department most frequently hires candidates who are: Completing or have completed a basic law enforcement training program. Sworn with other agencies and are seeking a change. However, the department occasionally hires and puts recruits through a basic law enforcement training program. Candidates who accept an offer to be hired as a recruit officer must agree to a two-year commitment to the Town of Hillsborough. Completing the hiring process can take several months. The process\xa0generally includes: Application — Hillsborough accepts applications through its employment website . Recruits will be required to submit all necessary documents, including: F-3 personal history statement Liability release Wri tten testing — Hillsborough uses a standardized, written, entry-level basic skills test\xa0in math, reading comprehension, grammar and incident report writing to help ensure candidates have the basic cognitive skills needed to perform the job. Physical assessment — Lateral candidates are asked to complete the\xa0Police Officer Physical Abilities Test\xa0within the state-mandated time. This requirement is waived if applicants can provide documentation that they have successfully completed the test within the past six months. Recruits will be required to complete a\xa0modified version of the Police Officer Physical Abilities Test to ensure candidates are adequately prepared to enter a basic law enforcement training program. Panel interview — Based on the results of written and physical testing, candidates will be invited to a panel interview. During this interview, three or four staff members will meet with the candidates and ask a series of questions. They will evaluate the candidates and provide a recommendation. Interview with the chief — Based on the results of the panel interview, the chief may conduct an additional interview with the candidate. This interview generally is conducted at the beginning of the process for lateral hires. Background investigation — Candidates who successfully complete the steps above will be moved as a group into the background investigation phase. The number referred varies depending on the number of openings and staff available to conduct background investigations. During the background investigation, an experienced investigator will meet with a candidate, verify all information provided and ensure no disqualifying factors or omissions exist. Conditional offer —\xa0The town will make a conditional job offer once the department is satisfied that a candidate: Has completed the hiring steps above. Is a person it wishes to hire. The conditional offer will outline salary, benefits and expectations.\xa0It is conditional upon the successful completion of some or all of the following: Drug screening Medical and psychological evaluations Polygraph testing where applicable Upon successfully completing the hiring process, a new candidate would be hired as a recruit officer and enrolled in a basic law enforcement training program at a nearby community college. The recruit officer would attend the program, and the department would provide all uniforms and materials for attending the program. Basic Law Enforcement Training We offer opportunities for interested people to enroll in and complete basic training through sponsorship or employment as a police recruit. About the Training Becoming a police officer in North Carolina requires successfully completing a basic law enforcement training program.\xa0The full-time program takes about 16-20 weeks to complete and concludes with a comprehensive written exam and skills testing. The program\xa0prepares entry-level individuals with the cognitive and physical skills needed to become certified law enforcement officers. It includes: 36 blocks of instruction with topics including: Arrest, search and seizure Driver training Firearms Motor vehicle law Practical exercises Extensive ethics training woven throughout the course. Minimum requirements to attend the basic training include being: A United States citizen. At least 20 years old. A high school graduate or having passed the General Educational Development tests. Being of good moral character. Basic law enforcement training programs are offered through many community colleges across the state. More information is available on the North Carolina Justice Academy website . Basic Training Sponsorship Individuals wishing to attended a BLET program can request that the Hillsborough Police Department sponsor them. Sponsorship allows the program’s $900 community college tuition to be waived. The individual is responsible for costs associated with books, uniforms and other supplies. Sponsorship is not an offer of employment, and neither the individual nor the agency is making a commitment. Those wishing to be considered for sponsorship must complete an application online through the town's employment website . All required documents must be submitted, including the F-3 personal history statement and the liability release. Those seeking to be considered for sponsorship must indicate this on the supplemental question that is part of the application. Sponsorship requests are considered on a case-by-case basis and generally do not involve a specific process and only minor background checks. Contact Information Police Station, 127 N. Churton St. 919-296-9500 919-732-2187 (fax) Office phones are answered 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Contact Police Department Staff Title Phone Email Chelenza, Nicholas Lieutenant 919-296-9523 Email Hooks, Richard Captain 919-296-9524 Email Simmons, Andy Assistant Chief of Police 919-296-9522 Email"]

Name: 8_patrol_traffic_worker_police
Representative_Docs: ['Patrol Functions Functions Patrol officers perform a wide range of patrol duties and community policing programs. In addition to responding to 911 calls for service and high-visibility patrol, they: Provide proactive traffic enforcement. Work with a social worker for services to help keep people out of the criminal justice system. In particular, they are responsible for: Apprehension of criminals and wanted persons High-visibility patrol and property checks Enforcement of motor vehicle laws Motor vehicle accidents Public and medical assistance Recovery of stolen property Community problem solving The officers also: Provide directed patrols and work with the public to deter crime. Provide field training for new officers who have completed N.C. basic law enforcement training until the officers meet standards to be certified.', "Patrol Functions Functions Patrol officers perform a wide range of patrol duties and community policing programs. In addition to responding to 911 calls for service and high-visibility patrol, they: Provide proactive traffic enforcement. Work with a social worker for services to help keep people out of the criminal justice system. In particular, they are responsible for: Apprehension of criminals and wanted persons High-visibility patrol and property checks Enforcement of motor vehicle laws Motor vehicle accidents Public and medical assistance Recovery of stolen property Community problem solving The officers also: Provide directed patrols and work with the public to deter crime. Provide field training for new officers who have completed N.C. basic law enforcement training until the officers meet standards to be certified. Patrol Tactics and Technologies Patrol Operations Officers use different tactics and technologies for patrol operations, including: Marked and unmarked vehicles. Mobile data terminals within the vehicles for access to local, state and national resources. Radar devices for conducting motor vehicle enforcement. Additionally, officers have: Basic first aid supplies in all primary patrol vehicles to assist with medical emergencies. Access to automated external defibrillators. Social Worker Services Yazmin Washington\xa0is a social worker working with the Hillsborough Police Department. The Police Department first added a social worker in October 2023  to help keep people with a history of serious mental illness from entering the criminal justice system. Yazmin Washington became the town's social worker in January 2025. She: Works with individuals both before an arrest is made and after criminal charges are brought. Provides resources and support to homeless and at-risk people. Works with trained crisis intervention officers to reduce the intensity of situations when mental health is a concern. Steps in after an event to connect individuals with services, such as transportation to doctors’ appointments and mental health counseling.", "Patrol officers are part of the Operations Division, which is staffed 24 hours daily. They are the most visible and accessible part of the Police Department. They serve as the front line protecting the community, responding to 911 calls for service and providing proactive high-visibility patrol. Patrol Functions Functions Patrol officers perform a wide range of patrol duties and community policing programs. In addition to responding to 911 calls for service and high-visibility patrol, they: Provide proactive traffic enforcement. Work with a social worker for services to help keep people out of the criminal justice system. In particular, they are responsible for: Apprehension of criminals and wanted persons High-visibility patrol and property checks Enforcement of motor vehicle laws Motor vehicle accidents Public and medical assistance Recovery of stolen property Community problem solving The officers also: Provide directed patrols and work with the public to deter crime. Provide field training for new officers who have completed N.C. basic law enforcement training until the officers meet standards to be certified. Patrol Tactics and Technologies Patrol Operations Officers use different tactics and technologies for patrol operations, including: Marked and unmarked vehicles. Mobile data terminals within the vehicles for access to local, state and national resources. Radar devices for conducting motor vehicle enforcement. Additionally, officers have: Basic first aid supplies in all primary patrol vehicles to assist with medical emergencies. Access to automated external defibrillators. Social Worker Services Yazmin Washington\xa0is a social worker working with the Hillsborough Police Department. The Police Department first added a social worker in October 2023  to help keep people with a history of serious mental illness from entering the criminal justice system. Yazmin Washington became the town's social worker in January 2025. She: Works with individuals both before an arrest is made and after criminal charges are brought. Provides resources and support to homeless and at-risk people. Works with trained crisis intervention officers to reduce the intensity of situations when mental health is a concern. Steps in after an event to connect individuals with services, such as transportation to doctors’ appointments and mental health counseling. Enforcement Activities Alcohol Law Enforcement The department has an alcohol law enforcement response team. The team c hecks for compliance throughout the year with: Operations that\xa0target sales to underage buyers at local businesses, including bars, restaurants and stores . Inspections of Alcohol Beverage Control permits at local businesses. Patrols dedicated to looking for impaired drivers. Traffic Enforcement Officers focus primarily on educating drivers. They place emphasize moving violations over nonmoving violations, such as those related to vehicle equipment or registration. Submit a Traffic Concern Safety Campaigns The Police Department participates in campaigns directed by the\xa0North Carolina Governor Highway Safety Program to reduce collisions, including campaigns on: Drinking and driving Seatbelts Speeding Speeding The department has digital signs for placement by roads to show drivers their current speed. One sign is moved around the town regularly. Contact Information Police Station, 127 N. Churton St. 919-296-9500 919-732-2187 (fax) Office phones are answered 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Call 911 for emergencies or to have an officer dispatched. Name: 9_historic_district_commission_application
Representative_Docs: ["Design Standards Exterior improvements to a site or structure in the Historic District may: Require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Historic District Commission. Be exempt from permitting. Be approvable as a minor works item by Planning and Economic Development Division staff. Before making any exterior changes to a site or structure, discuss the proposal with town staff to determine the process required for the project and to avoid potential after-the-fact permit penalties and enforcement. These standards and matrices guide the commission's decisions on Certificate of Appropriateness applications: Compatibility Matrix Historic District Design Standards (effective Sept. 3, 2021) Historic District Signage Matrix", "The Historic District Commission reviews applications for exterior changes within the Hillsborough Historic District. A Certificate of Appropriateness or minor works approval from staff may be required before any exterior work can begin. All meetings are open to the public. The Historic District Commission approves changes to the district, including the addition of interpretive signs along the Riverwalk greenway. Agendas and Minutes Certificate of Appropriateness About the Historic District The Hillsborough Historic District was created in 1973 through passage of a preservation ordinance. The district presents a visual history of Hillsborough's development and\xa0includes the town’s commercial core and surrounding residential neighborhoods. The district is\xa0listed on the National Register of Historic Places and\xa0contains more than 100 houses, churches and buildings from the 18th and 19th centuries. Included is development from Georgian- and Federal-style buildings dating to the late 1700s to contemporary construction. The town's historic district overlaps with the National Register of Historic Places district, but it is a separate designation. Historic District Inventory Historic District Map About the Historic District Commission Mission The commission’s mission is to identify, protect and preserve Hillsborough's architectural resources and to educate the public about those resources and preservation in general. It conducts an awards program for noteworthy historic preservation projects in town. Role The commission has a quasi-judicial role. The commission: Hears evidence. Determines relevant facts. Ensures the changes are\xa0not conflicting with the special character of the historic district or landmark. Regulation It is against the law to begin building, moving, demolishing, altering or restoring any structure or site within the Historic District before a\xa0Certificate of Appropriateness\xa0has been issued by the commission or approved by staff as a minor work or ordinary maintenance and repair.\xa0See the Design Standards section on this page. Design Standards Exterior improvements to a site or structure in the Historic District may: Require a Certificate of Appropriateness from the Historic District Commission. Be exempt from permitting. Be approvable as a minor works item by Planning and Economic Development Division staff. Before making any exterior changes to a site or structure, discuss the proposal with town staff to determine the process required for the project and to avoid potential after-the-fact permit penalties and enforcement. These standards and matrices guide the commission's decisions on Certificate of Appropriateness applications: Compatibility Matrix Historic District Design Standards (effective Sept. 3, 2021) Historic District Signage Matrix Membership Composition A majority of the commission’s members have specialized training or an interest in architecture, preservation, archaeology, history or building methods. The district is\xa0listed on the National Register of Historic Places and\xa0contains more than 100 houses, churches and buildings from the 18th and 19th centuries. The town's historic district overlaps with the National Register of Historic Places district, but it is a separate designation. Name: 10_limits_county_orange_jurisdiction
Representative_Docs: ["Services Outside Town Limits Services Outside Town Limits The town has zoning jurisdiction within a limited area beyond town limits known as the extraterritorial jurisdiction area. For properties outside town limits, including those within Hillsborough's extraterritorial jurisdiction area, the town: May or may not provide water and sewer service . See the section on the water and sewer service area. Has a courtesy review agreement with Orange County for development projects and can provide recommendations. Charges fees for any connections to its water and sewer system. Fully reviews and determines approval of construction plans for any connections within a development project to its water and sewer system. This may include execution of a water and sewer extension contract. Other agencies provide safety and solid waste services: Private companies provide garbage collection. Orange County provides drop-off sites and recycling collection . Orange County Sheriff's Office provides law enforcement . Orange Rural Fire Department provides fire protection . Orange County oversees zoning for properties outside town limits and outside the town's extraterritorial jurisdiction area.", "Many people have a Hillsborough mailing address but do not live in town limits. Additionally, the town limits do not match Hillsborough's planning jurisdiction or water and sewer service areas. Find information about a property and the services offered there\xa0through the Orange County interactive zoning website . See the instructions below. Finding a Property Finding a Property To find a property on the zoning website: Enter the street address, owner name or parcel identification number in the appropriate search box. Click the Search button. The map will zoom to the location. Property information will appear in the Results tab on the left. If the website returns “0 records found,” your information may be out of date. Map Color Codes Dark Gray Background ― The property is in town limits. Light\xa0Gray\xa0Background ― The property is outside town limits but in the extraterritorial jurisdiction area. See the section on services outside town limits. Colored\xa0Background ― The property is outside town limits and the extraterritorial jurisdiction area. Zoom out of the map to better distinguish between the gray areas. Services Within Town Limits Services Within Town Limits If a property is within town limits, the town: Provides garbage collection . Provides law enforcement . Provides water and sewer service , if available. Fees and approval are required for any connections to the town's water and sewer system. Complete an availability inquiry to learn if water and sewer service is available. Oversees zoning. See the Zoning Compliance Permits page for more information and an interactive zoning map. Other agencies provide services in the town limits: Orange County provides recycling collection . Orange Rural Fire Department provides fire protection . Services Outside Town Limits Services Outside Town Limits The town has zoning jurisdiction within a limited area beyond town limits known as the extraterritorial jurisdiction area. For properties outside town limits, including those within Hillsborough's extraterritorial jurisdiction area, the town: May or may not provide water and sewer service . See the section on the water and sewer service area. Has a courtesy review agreement with Orange County for development projects and can provide recommendations. Charges fees for any connections to its water and sewer system. Fully reviews and determines approval of construction plans for any connections within a development project to its water and sewer system. This may include execution of a water and sewer extension contract. Other agencies provide safety and solid waste services: Private companies provide garbage collection. Orange County provides drop-off sites and recycling collection . Orange County Sheriff's Office provides law enforcement . Orange Rural Fire Department provides fire protection . Orange County oversees zoning for properties outside town limits and outside the town's extraterritorial jurisdiction area. Water and Sewer Service Area Water and Sewer Service Area The town's water and sewer service area extends beyond town limits. Once a property is found on the zoning website, do the following to determine whether it falls within the water and sewer service area: Click the Layers tab. Check the Water Sewer Jurisdictional Boundary box. Read the service designation for the property. Service Designations Hillsborough Primary Service Area ― Town water and sewer service may be available but not everywhere in this area. The town's comprehensive sustainability plan recommends reducing this area and following the plan's recommended development area. Hillsborough Long-Term Service Area ― Town water and sewer service does not extend into this area. Hillsborough is not focusing on expanding its system into this area. Service For questions about whether town water and sewer service is available for a specific property, complete the Water and Sewer Availability Inquiry . Name: 11_sewer_customers_rates_cost
Representative_Docs: ['The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated.', 'Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated.', "Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated."]

It also maintains pipes and infrastructure to deliver drinking water and transport wastewater for treatment. See the Water and Sewer Services page for billing, rates, service connection and other services related to the water and sewer system. West Fork Eno Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's source for water. No recreation is allowed at the reservoir. Learn More Tour our water plant See what it takes to prepare water from the Eno River for drinking. Sign Up Tour our wastewater plant What happens to the water you use? See how the town cleans the water for return to the Eno River. Sign Up Annual Reports Hillsborough's treatment plants regularly test for contaminants and prepare annual reports about their treatment processes and testing. Water Quality Report An annual report is published in July\xa0for the previous year. It includes  information on our water treatment system and performance as well as water quality information from the Environmental Protection Agency. Water Quality Report Wastewater Quality Report A report is published in August each year for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). It includes information on our wastewater collection and treatment systems and their performance. Wastewater Quality Report Distribution and Collection Division The Water Distribution and Wastewater Collection Division: Repairs water line leaks. Cleans and repairs sewer lines. Maintains water tanks, booster pump stations and sewage lift stations. Staff members are responsible for programs and projects to maintain and protect the utility system. See: About Water About Wastewater Treatment Plants The town’s treatment plants prepare drinking water and clean used water. They regularly test for contaminants. Water Treatment Plant The water plant takes water from the Eno River, cleans and disinfects it, and then pumps it to customers. The state requires that the plant change its disinfection method for one month each year to clean the distribution system. About Water Wastewater Treatment Plant The wastewater plant removes waste and nutrients from used water, disinfects the water with chlorine, and then removes the chlorine before releasing the water into the Eno River. Name: 13_shelters_weather_shelter_cold
Representative_Docs: ["Low temperatures can bring power outages due to high electrical demand. Stay safe with these tips: Heater Safety If heat is out, go to a neighbor or family member’s home if possible. Do not use generators, grills, camp stoves or other gasoline or charcoal-burning devices inside\xa0the home, basement or garage or near a window. These devices create carbon monoxide gas,\xa0which is poisonous. Check heating units and carbon monoxide detectors. Poorly operating or damaged heating units\xa0can release carbon monoxide gas. Caring for Others Don't leave children, elderly people or pets alone in vehicles. Temperatures in vehicles can drop rapidly.\xa0Pets can be greatly affected by the cold and should not be exposed long. Remember that older adults, people in fragile health and small children are affected more by\xa0the cold than the average adult. Check on loved ones and neighbors, preferably by telephone. Caring for Yourself Create a warm room by sealing off the warmest room in the home where everyone gathers. Close off additional rooms, cover doorways and windows, and block drafts with towels, blankets or clothes. Avoid unnecessarily opening doors and windows. Keep body heat in by layering loose but warm clothing, wearing a hat, using blankets, and huddling with people or pets. Eat and drink regularly to help stay warm. Avoid alcohol and caffeine because they cause the body to lose heat faster. Wear appropriate clothing that will adequately insulate from the cold when outdoors. The\xa0National Weather Service advises wearing a hat and gloves if outdoors during a wind chill\xa0advisory. Avoid\xa0overexertion and be aware of the amount and intensity of physical activity indoors and outdoors. Cold weather strains the body, especially the heart. Resources Personal Safety in Extreme Cold and Power Outages — Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Fire Safety Tips — National Fire Protection Association Food Safety After Power Outages — U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Disaster Response — American Red Cross in English or Español State Road Information — 511 Information Line", "Below is information on how the town prepares for winter weather and how you can too. Many people have a Hillsborough mailing address but do not live in town limits. See the Services for Your Property page. Town Activities Preparations In advance of predicted winter weather, Hillsborough crews prepare equipment and resources, including: Outfitting trucks with plows and tire chains. Ensuring vehicles and generators are fully fueled. Ensuring any equipment that may be needed is ready to go. Pretreating some roadways with brine if needed, including Rainey Avenue; Beckett’s Ridge, Lafayette, Lakeshore, Meadowlands and Waterstone drives; East Margaret Lane; College Park Road; and Cameron Street. Operations Snow and Ice Removal Visit the Street Maintenance page to view plowing guidelines and the town's responsibilities for winter weather. Curbside Collections Hillsborough will stop curbside collections of trash, brush, bulk items and loose leaves if weather conditions prevent collection trucks from operating safely. Garbage collection will be given priority when collections resume. Orange County provides collection of recyclables. See the Orange County website for changes in its services. Parks and Trails Parks and greenways are not a priority for snow removal. When weather conditions deteriorate, staff will close parks and greenways until they determine the areas are safe. Please heed all safety signage and barricades and do not enter closed areas. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. Entering closed areas or moving signs and barriers also puts others at risk and pulls emergency workers away from other duties. Community Responsibilities Preparation When weather conditions worsen, please: Stay off roads, sidewalks and town trails. Do not park vehicles on streets because this affects plowing. Do not pass vehicles plowing snow or spreading salt or brine to avoid bodily injury or damage to vehicle paint. Remember that sidewalks and roads may continue to have snow and ice and to be hazardous after a storm, particularly in shaded areas. Traveling on roads, sidewalks and trails during severe weather is risky, creates more emergency work, and slows efforts to keep roadways cleared for emergency response. Resources Emergency Information See emergency information on the Ready Hillsborough page, including key local contacts, how to report problems and how to subscribe to emergency notifications. Emergency Kits Basic readiness tips include having adequate supplies of food and water to last several days and putting together a disaster supply kit, including a flashlight, batteries, cash, first aid supplies and copies of\xa0critical documents. See the Ready Hillsborough page. Equipment and Vehicles Prepare your equipment and vehicles by ensuring they are fully fueled. Additionally, vehicles may be equipped with tire chains\xa0to improve traction and prevent slipping on snowy or icy roads. Stay Informed Please stay informed and share information with neighbors. Use the Town of Hillsborough’s various communication channels: Website\xa0— hillsboroughnc.gov News releases\xa0— Get News page to receive town news by email Facebook\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Instagram\xa0— @HillsboroughGov Nextdoor\xa0— www.nextdoor.com/pages/town-of-hillsborough-2 Bluesky — @hillsboroughnc.gov Safety Tips Low temperatures can bring power outages due to high electrical demand. Stay safe with these tips: Heater Safety If heat is out, go to a neighbor or family member’s home if possible. Do not use generators, grills, camp stoves or other gasoline or charcoal-burning devices inside\xa0the home, basement or garage or near a window. These devices create carbon monoxide gas,\xa0which is poisonous. Check heating units and carbon monoxide detectors. Poorly operating or damaged heating units\xa0can release carbon monoxide gas. Caring for Others Don't leave children, elderly people or pets alone in vehicles. Temperatures in vehicles can drop rapidly.\xa0Pets can be greatly affected by the cold and should not be exposed long. Remember that older adults, people in fragile health and small children are affected more by\xa0the cold than the average adult. Check on loved ones and neighbors, preferably by telephone. Caring for Yourself Create a warm room by sealing off the warmest room in the home where everyone gathers. Close off additional rooms, cover doorways and windows, and block drafts with towels, blankets or clothes. Avoid unnecessarily opening doors and windows. Keep body heat in by layering loose but warm clothing, wearing a hat, using blankets, and huddling with people or pets. Eat and drink regularly to help stay warm. Avoid alcohol and caffeine because they cause the body to lose heat faster. Wear appropriate clothing that will adequately insulate from the cold when outdoors. The\xa0National Weather Service advises wearing a hat and gloves if outdoors during a wind chill\xa0advisory. Avoid\xa0overexertion and be aware of the amount and intensity of physical activity indoors and outdoors. Cold weather strains the body, especially the heart. Resources Personal Safety in Extreme Cold and Power Outages — Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Fire Safety Tips — National Fire Protection Association Food Safety After Power Outages — U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Disaster Response — American Red Cross in English or Español State Road Information — 511 Information Line Pipes and Cold Weather Frozen pipes can burst and cause substantial damage and water loss. This includes fire sprinkler systems. See the Backflow Prevention page for information on protecting backflow prevention devices in winter. Preventing Frozen and Burst Pipes Use these tips to protect water pipes during freezing temperatures. Unheated Areas Pipes in unheated areas have the greatest chance of freezing. Unheated areas include attics, basements and crawl spaces. Insulate pipes ― Pipe insulation is available in fiberglass or foam sleeves.\xa0Home centers and hardware stores have sleeves providing one-eighth to five-eighths inches of insulation. Specialty dealers have products that provide up to 2 inches of insulation. The extra thickness can save a pipe. Heat pipes ―\xa0Heating cables and tapes are effective. Select a heating cable with the UL label and a built-in thermostat that turns\xa0the heat on when needed. Follow the manufacturer’s instructions closely. If you have it, turn on an incandescent\xa0light bulb in your crawl space for extra warmth. Seal openings and air leaks ― This includes access doors, air vents in the foundation, and cracks.\xa0Especially keep cold wind away from pipes, which speeds the freezing process. Close and seal cracks and other openings with insulation, cardboard, plastic or newspaper. Foundation air vents may have hinged or sliding covers to easily close the vents. Ensure your water meter box cover is closed ― The cover prevents cold air from freezing water inside the meter. If the cover is broken or missing and you are a Hillsborough water customer, call the Town of Hillsborough at 919-732-3621. Heated Areas Leave cabinet doors open under kitchen and bathroom sinks ― This\xa0allows warmer room air to\xa0circulate around pipes. Let water drip slowly from the highest faucet in the building ― Use this tip if you have difficulty protecting pipes in an unheated area or if you believe your pipes may freeze for other reasons. An open faucet allows water to escape before pressure builds to a point where a pipe can burst. If dripping stops, ice may be blocking the pipe. Keep the\xa0faucet open because the pipe still needs pressure relief. Frozen and Burst Pipes If Your Pipes Are Frozen If nothing comes out of your faucets: Leave the faucets open and call a plumber. Try to thaw the pipe with warm air from a hair dryer ― Start by warming the pipe as close to the faucet as possible. Then work toward the coldest section of pipe. Do not use electrical appliances in areas of standing water. You could be electrocuted. Do not use a torch or other open flame. This could cause a fire hazard. If Your Pipes Have Burst Turn off water at the main shutoff valve and leave faucets open\xa0― Make sure everyone knows where the master valve is for your plumbing system and how to open and close it. The valve may be in your basement, crawl space or closet where water pipes enter the house or building. If you do not have a master shutoff valve,\xa0consider installing one. Drain the pipes in your plumbing system ― Open the highest and lowest cold-water faucets in your house until the water is depleted. Before draining pipes\xa0― Turn off the water heater to ensure the lack of water does not burn out the\xa0heating elements. After draining pipes ―\xa0Before turning on the water heater again, ensure water service is restored and the water tank is refilled. If you fix a leak, you may qualify for an adjustment to your utility bill. See the Billing and Adjustments page.", "Safety Tips Low temperatures can bring power outages due to high electrical demand.
