Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. Water Quality Concerns For information on the quality of your water, see\xa0the annual Water Quality Report . If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Whole House Filters Whole house water filter systems are devices that customers can buy privately to treat their water. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers. Contact Information Water Distribution Division Adron Thompson Water/Sewer Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Water Treatment Plant, 711 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-732-3621 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Cates, Nathan Water Treatment Plant Superintendent 919-296-9641 Email Lashley, Joel Utility System Superintendent 919-296-9651 Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email", "Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Before the water is safe to drink, it must go through multiple stages of treatment, which can take several days. The Water Treatment Plant is staffed 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Staff who maintain the pipes, pumps and tanks connected with supplying water to you must be available at all hours for emergencies. About Water Alerts Backflow Prevention Lead and Drinking Water Water Conservation Our Drinking Water Source Eno River, Lakes and Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's water source. Water from the river is drawn from three sites located in the Upper Neuse River watershed. The Eno and these three sites are considered surface water\xa0— natural water that has not penetrated much below the ground. Lakes on the Eno River Lake Ben Johnston\xa0— Hillsborough takes water from this lake to make drinking water. Seven Mile Creek enters the Eno River upstream of this area. Lake Orange —\xa0The town uses this lake as a secondary source of water from the Eno. The lake is on the east fork of the river outside town limits. It is maintained by Orange County. West Fork Eno Reservoir The town owns this reservoir on the west fork of the Eno River in nearby Cedar Grove and uses it as a secondary source for water.\xa0It can store about 1.2 billion gallons of water. The reservoir opened in 2000, and a second\xa0phase was completed in 2021.\xa0It includes an innovative spillway that provides more surface in a compact area for water to spill over. The dam resembles piano keys when in use. Emergency Water The town has pipe connections with neighboring utilities to receive or send water during emergencies. Receiving Water Hillsborough can receive water from Durham in an emergency. Additionally, the Hillsborough Utilities Department is working on a project that would enable the town to receive water from its southern utilities neighbor, Orange Water and Sewer Authority. Sending Water Hillsborough can send water to: City of Durham Water Management Orange-Alamance Water System, which serves some rural areas in Orange and Alamance counties Orange Water and Sewer Authority, which serves the Carrboro and Chapel Hill area History of the Reservoir In the 1980s and early 1990s, the Hillsborough community began exploring options for addressing the town’s water shortage due to a history of droughts and water restrictions and a desire to accommodate expected future growth. In 1993, Hillsborough voters approved general obligation bonds to help pay for building the reservoir’s first phase. The first phase was built with expansion in mind. The reservoir’s 20-year permit allowed for a second phase to be started by 2018. In 2000, the reservoir was made operational earlier than scheduled and before full to help provide immediate drought relief for customers under water restrictions. Severe Droughts Hillsborough experienced severe droughts in the 1920s, 1930s, 1950s, 1980s and 2000s. In 1954, schools closed at noon and the town sought federal aid because of drought conditions. That same year, drought conditions forced the town to disconnect the water of any customer found wasting it. In 1988, mandatory water restrictions were put in place. By the early 1990s, the town completed an interconnected pipeline with the City of Durham to allow regular purchases from Durham’s water system to withstand periods of droughts and water restrictions. The town could only purchase water between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. due to demands on Durham’s system. Benefits Phase 1 of the reservoir has: Helped the town withstand several severe droughts in the 2000s. At one point, larger surrounding systems were near or below 50 days of remaining water, but Hillsborough still had enough water for 200 days. Allowed Hillsborough to accommodate new development. Many residents and businesses joining Hillsborough after 2000 would not be here today without the reservoir. The Phase 2 expansion of the reservoir: Is a long-term investment in Hillsborough’s water supply. Raised the reservoir about 10 feet, doubling its volume and providing a maximum yield of 6 million gallons of water per day. No Recreation on the Reservoir Recreation is not allowed at the West Fork Eno Reservoir or the surrounding town-owned property. This includes boating, hunting and fishing. Required Conservation Federal and state permits require a conservation easement to: Protect the town’s drinking water source. Reduce the effects of the reservoir on others. To provide\xa0a 100-foot buffer around the reservoir, the town must keep the land in a natural state with no development, recreation or access other than to maintain the reservoir. Risks The town does not have the resources to oversee public activities at the reservoir and cannot assume the risk or liability of allowing recreational activities. Recreation also could introduce invasive animals and plants. The town already funds treatment to remove a weed native to South Asia that can damage utility equipment and the environment. See the section on annual treatment for an invasive plant on this page. Seasonal Treatment for Hydrilla The state does not plan to treat the Eno River for hydrilla in 2026 due to the flooding from Tropical Storm Chantal in 2025. A survey will be conducted later this year to determine whether hydrilla treatment should resume. Typically, the state annually treats 16 miles of the Eno River with the herbicide fluridone to reduce hydrilla, an invasive aquatic plant.\xa0Applications typically start in mid-May and continue through August. The section of the river treated is\xa0below the Ben Johnston Lake dam in Orange County to Roxboro Road and U.S. 501 in Durham County. The herbicide is applied in a concentration that is safe for humans, fish and wildlife and\xa0well below limits set by the Environmental Protection Agency. Property owners next to the treated section of river should use caution in using river water for irrigation during this time. About Hydrilla Hydrilla is native to South Asia and grows rapidly, creating thick mats on the surfaces of lakes, rivers and other waterways. The weed: Negatively affects the health of fish and wildlife in the area. Can clog intakes for supplying drinking water and irrigation. Can harbor mosquitoes. Can interfere with recreation on waterways. The plant was discovered in the Eno River watershed in the early 1990s in Lake Orange, which is upstream of Hillsborough and one of the town's water sources. In 2009, biologists confirmed hydrilla in the West Fork Eno Reservoir. The town-owned reservoir is stocked with triploid grass carp to manage the invasive plant. More Information The North Carolina Division of Water Resources manages the annual treatment. Hillsborough helps fund it. For more information, contact Stormwater and Environmental Services Manager Terry Hackett by email or phone at 919-296-9621. Water Treatment Our Water Quality The town's water treatment must meet high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than 150 substances in the town's drinking water and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Water Quality Report . Water Plant Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has: A state-certified laboratory to test for bacteria daily. Two lab-certified operators who test raw and finished water at the plant every two hours\xa0— more than required by the state. Water System Testing Staff also test various points in the water system. The water system includes the pipes, pumps and tanks needed to transport and store water. Tests occur: Weekly\xa0— For bacteria at five of the farthest points in the town's water lines. Monthly — For bacteria at 15 areas. Quarterly — For disinfection byproducts. These are substances created by the process to clean water. Annually — For inorganic materials, pesticides, synthetic organic chemicals, nitrates, volatile organic compounds and secondary contaminants. Every five years — For unregulated contaminants that the Environmental Protection Agency lists for testing. Our Water Treatment Process The Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant is the processing plant for your water. The plant takes raw water from the Eno River. It then adds ingredients and applies processes to the water to make it usable. The plant focuses on: First\xa0— Cleanliness and compliance with standards. Second\xa0— Taste, odor and appearance. Water Treatment Converting raw water into drinking water includes injecting chemicals at multiple points to help remove organic\xa0material, disinfect the water and ensure longer freshness before releasing it for use by the community. The\xa0chemical process starts before the raw water reaches the Water Treatment Plant. Water released from the town's reservoir travels to Lake Ben Johnston, where the town removes water for use.\xa0At this intake location, a chemical is added to help particles in the water combine for easier removal. At the plant, more\xa0chemicals are added for the same reason and to adjust the water's color,\xa0acidity and alkalinity. Filtering Out Materials The water then goes through several filtration, aeration and clarification stages. In these stages, the water remains in large basins while being mixed at slower and slower speeds. This allows the combined large materials to drop to the bottom of the basins. Finally, the clear water\xa0moves through a fine filtration system to remove remaining particles.\xa0At this stage, the water’s turbidity is measured and filtration\xa0adjustments are made to remove any cloudiness from the water caused by large amounts of particles that\xa0are otherwise invisible to the eye. Adjusting Treatment The water is then disinfected and final adjustments to acidity and\xa0alkalinity are made before the water is pumped through pipes to homes and businesses. Throughout the\xa0treatment process, employees test the water for more than 150 contaminants, making adjustments to the\xa0treatment as needed. See: From River to Faucet: Hillsborough's Water Treatment Process video Water Treatment Plant and Tours The Water Treatment Plant employees are highly certified. They continually operate and maintain the plant 24 hours a day to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of drinking water per day. Awards In previous years, the Water Treatment Plant and its staff won an award for Outstanding Operator of the Year from the N.C. Waterworks Operators Association. Tours See what it takes to prepare water taken from the Eno River for drinking. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require complete a request form. Request a Tour Water Distribution  and System Maintenance Annual Chlorine Disinfection For one month each year, the town is required to disinfect drinking water with chlorine. The state requires this annual change in utilities that normally use chloramines,\xa0a compound of chlorine and\xa0ammonia. Chlorine is a more intense disinfectant,\xa0and\xa0the annual change helps ensure\xa0a high level of disinfection in water mains. Other utilities in the region conduct the disinfection at the same time. This includes Hillsborough’s\xa0interconnected neighboring utilities, Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the City of Durham. Effects on Customers During the annual disinfection, some customers may notice a chlorine taste or odor in Hillsborough’s drinking water. Your water remains safe to drink. To remove chlorine taste and odor from water, you can: Add a few lemon slices to a pitcher of water. The lemon’s ascorbic acid will neutralize the chlorine. Let water sit in an open container in the refrigerator or on a counter for a day or so. A wide or shallow container will allow more contact between the air and water. Filter water with activated carbon. Water pitchers with such filters are sold locally. Boil water for 15 minutes to evaporate the chlorine. If you use Hillsborough water for special purposes or for processes involving careful control of water\xa0characteristics, the town encourages you to seek advice from an appropriate technical source — such\xa0as a filter vendor or service company — about whether and how to make adjustments to your use of\xa0Hillsborough water during the month of chlorine disinfection. Annual and Periodic Water System Flushing During the annual disinfection, staff release water from fire hydrants to ensure chlorine flows through the entire system. They also perform maintenance on hydrants. The annual flushing helps remove sediment and other minerals that may have settled in the water system.\xa0This flushing can cause temporary cloudiness or discoloration of water and could discolor light-colored fabric.\xa0If discoloration or air bubbles appear in the water, customers should run cold water in a bathtub for a few minutes until the water and air bubbles clear. If the water does not clear within five minutes, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. During flushing or hydrant maintenance: Town personnel may be required to trim plantings or remove other items to ensure that adequate access to the hydrants exists for emergencies and maintenance. Community members may be asked to relocate plants and other items in street rights-of-way where necessary to provide a clear, 3-foot-wide area around a hydrant. Water Line Breaks The responsibility for repairs of water lines depends on whether the water line is on the utility or the property owner's side of the water meter or service connection. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the water main. The supply system's main line typically is located under roadways. The town also maintains water service connections in public rights of ways or easements. It will make repairs to the line in that area. Property owners are responsible for the water service line between their property's house or structure and the water meter or service connection. Water Meter A property's water meter typically indicates the ownership line. The meter is placed on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way or easement. If a water meter is not located in the public right of way or easement,\xa0the town will provide water service maintenance to the meter and may require relocation during redevelopment. Water Distribution Division The Water Distribution Division repairs water line breaks and maintains: 153 miles of water lines 3 water boosting pump stations 5 water tanks The division includes a backflow prevention program to prevent contaminants from entering the water supply. Awards In past years, the division has won\xa0Outstanding Water Distribution Operator of the Year from the N.C. American Water Works Association-Water Environment Association. More Information Black Residue Black residue around utility fixtures generally does not indicate a problem with water quality. It is likely due to one of the following: Bacteria feeding on oxidized iron and manganese in water. A reaction between ammonia that the town uses for disinfection and rubber in faucet rings or toilet flaps. Iron and manganese are naturally occurring minerals and are in most water across the United States. The bacteria that feed on iron and manganese are non-health threatening. As they feed on the minerals, they form red-brown or black-brown residue that can clog water systems and cause a foul odor. Hillsborough and neighboring utilities use chloramines — a compound of chlorine and ammonia — to disinfect and preserve water. Removal To remove the iron and manganese bacteria, use equal parts of white vinegar, peroxide and water to clean any residue seen. This solution can be used periodically as a preventative measure on utility fixtures. To resolve the issue of a reaction between ammonia and rubber, the rubber materials in faucets and toilets can be replaced with materials that can withstand ammonia. Check labels on replacement products. To request testing or to make utilities crews aware of the issue and location, call the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. Crews can take a water sample from the home to determine the cause and\xa0discuss solutions. Pink Residue A pink to reddish film or residue on bathroom and kitchen surfaces is likely the result of airborne bacteria in the home that produce a pinkish or dark gray film on routinely moist surfaces, such as toilet bowls, showerheads, sink drains and tiles. The bacterium is likely Serratia marcescens , which is found naturally in soil, food and animals. It produces a red pigment and thrives on moisture, dust and phosphates, such as soap and shampoo\xa0residue. The bacteria have been tied to urinary tract infections, wound infections and respiratory problems in some people. Removal To prevent the pink residue: Keep bathtubs and sinks wiped down and dry. Clean surfaces with a solution that contains chlorine. Periodically disinfect toilets with chlorine bleach. Note that abrasive cleaners may scratch fixtures and allow increased growth of bacteria. Stale Water Before resuming use of a building or during periods of underutilized use, remove stale and potentially unhealthy water in your building’s water system by\xa0flushing the water system and all water devices. For more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant. Background When buildings are vacant or operating at significantly reduced capacity for more than a few days, water sits in pipes and devices instead of flowing. The chlorine and ammonia that the\xa0Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant adds to disinfect water disappears over time.\xa0Without proper levels of disinfectant, disease-causing microorganisms can begin to grow. Corrosion control also can be affected. Flushing Water Systems and Devices Flushing in stages is best. Begin at the main service line and include all the plumbing, storage tanks, fixtures, fire suppression systems, and equipment such as water fountains and ice machines. The first flush pulls out stale water. Follow-up flushes ensure fresh water with disinfectant is drawn fully through the building. For larger buildings, a single flush isn’t enough to reestablish good water quality. The longer service is interrupted, the more flushing is needed. Depending on the complexity of a building’s water system, facility managers should develop a comprehensive water management program for this process, but it should not replace a facility-specific Water Management Plan. Flushing Steps to Consider In addition to the following steps, consider capturing and reusing flushed water for outdoor watering or cleaning purposes. Flush all interior and exterior faucets (remove faucet aerators, if possible) for 10 to 30 minutes. Open all outlets at once to flush the service line and then open them again, individually, starting near where the water enters the building. Flush cold water first. Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. Water Quality Concerns For information on the quality of your water, see\xa0the annual Water Quality Report . If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Whole House Filters Whole house water filter systems are devices that customers can buy privately to treat their water. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers. Contact Information Water Distribution Division Adron Thompson Water/Sewer Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Water Treatment Plant, 711 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-732-3621 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Representative_Docs: ['Comments Policy Comments Policy Please note that Town of Hillsborough social media sites are moderated online discussion sites and are subject to North Carolina public records laws and electronic discovery laws and policies. All content posted by the public to the town’s social media sites should be related to discussion of town-sponsored programs, services, projects, issues, events and activities.\xa0All comments and content shared on our government-sponsored social media sites must pertain to items within the jurisdictional control or authority of our governmental unit. The Town of Hillsborough reserves the right to delete submissions that do not meet the purpose of its social media sites. The following list includes examples of unacceptable social networking content and comments. It is not\xa0intended to be all-inclusive. Content or comments that are spam or not topically related to town-sponsored programs, services, projects, issues, events and activities or the particular post being commented on. This includes comments not related to the jurisdiction or to the authority of the jurisdiction. Profane or obscene language or content. Such language or content is subject to being blocked or removed by the social media platform’s filtering functions. This includes sexual content or links to sexual content. Content that promotes, fosters or perpetuates discrimination on the basis of race; creed; color; age; religion; gender; gender identity; gender expression; marital status; status with regard to public assistance; national origin; physical or mental disability; sexual orientation; or any other protected class. Content that advocates violence or illegal activity or compromises the safety and security of the public or public system. Confidential or nonpublic information, including any personally identifiable medical information or content that violates the legal rights of the owner and said content. Promotions or content related to political campaigns or organizations. Solicitations of commerce, including content that contains gratuitous links (links or references to other relevant governmental webpages or official government sites are acceptable). Infringement on copyrights or trademarks. Duplicate posts by the same author (the original post will be left intact assuming it follows the guidelines of acceptable comments or content). The Town of Hillsborough also reserves the right to disallow comments altogether. This policy is subject to amendment or modification at any time.', "The Town of Hillsborough's social media sites are intended to provide community members and visitors with timely information about our services, programs and emergency communications. The Communications Division oversees the town's social media sites and policies. Policies and Disclaimers Disclaimer Statement Disclaimer Statement Representatives of the Town of Hillsborough government communicate via town social media sites. Consequently, any communication on our social media site is subject to North Carolina public records law. Any comment, feedback or other method of communication made by someone other than an official representative of the Town of Hillsborough is solely the view and opinion of the author. It does not represent an official communication from the Town of Hillsborough or any member of its staff. The Town of Hillsborough does not endorse any advertisement or marketing effort that may be seen on our social media sites. Contact Statement Contact Statement We value your comments and feedback but are unable to answer questions and concerns via our social media forums. Please visit  the Contact Us page. Comments Policy Comments Policy Please note that Town of Hillsborough social media sites are moderated online discussion sites and are subject to North Carolina public records laws and electronic discovery laws and policies. All content posted by the public to the town’s social media sites should be related to discussion of town-sponsored programs, services, projects, issues, events and activities.\xa0All comments and content shared on our government-sponsored social media sites must pertain to items within the jurisdictional control or authority of our governmental unit. The Town of Hillsborough reserves the right to delete submissions that do not meet the purpose of its social media sites. Content or comments that are spam or not topically related to town-sponsored programs, services, projects, issues, events and activities or the particular post being commented on. This includes comments not related to the jurisdiction or to the authority of the jurisdiction. Profane or obscene language or content. Such language or content is subject to being blocked or removed by the social media platform’s filtering functions. This includes sexual content or links to sexual content. Content that promotes, fosters or perpetuates discrimination on the basis of race; creed; color; age; religion; gender; gender identity; gender expression; marital status; status with regard to public assistance; national origin; physical or mental disability; sexual orientation; or any other protected class. Content that advocates violence or illegal activity or compromises the safety and security of the public or public system. Confidential or nonpublic information, including any personally identifiable medical information or content that violates the legal rights of the owner and said content. Promotions or content related to political campaigns or organizations. Solicitations of commerce, including content that contains gratuitous links (links or references to other relevant governmental webpages or official government sites are acceptable). Infringement on copyrights or trademarks. Duplicate posts by the same author (the original post will be left intact assuming it follows the guidelines of acceptable comments or content). The Town of Hillsborough also reserves the right to disallow comments altogether. This policy is subject to amendment or modification at any time. Comments Disclaimer Statement Comments Disclaimer Statement Please do not make requests for emergency service or for immediate response through social media. Use the Town of Hillsborough website for direct contact to town departments. Contact Us page includes phone numbers for emergency water and sewer service. Call 911 to file a police report, report an emergency or request an officer. Any comment, feedback or other method of communication made by someone other than an official Town of Hillsborough representative is solely author's view and opinion. It does not represent an official communication or a reflection of the opinions and position of the town and its staff. The Town of Hillsborough does not endorse any advertisement or marketing effort that may be seen on a social media site.\xa0Comments on town social media sites may occasionally contain spam, scam, phishing or bot-generated links posted by third parties. These links are not endorsed, verified or approved by the town. Please use caution before clicking any link or providing personal information online. When in doubt, visit official websites directly rather than through links posted in comments. Suspicious comments should be reported to the town and directly to the social media platform. Name: 38_wastewater_lake_falls_treatment
Representative_Docs: ["Hillsborough's treated wastewater is released into the Eno River. Before the water is safe to return to the river, it must go through multiple stages of treatment at the Wastewater Treatment Plant. The Eno River is within the Upper Neuse River Basin, an ecologically sensitive area. Due to this, the state imposes strict requirements on treated wastewater. Staff who maintain the pipes and pumps connected with removing your used water must be available at all hours for emergencies. The Upper Neuse River Watershed is the 770-square-mile area draining to the Falls Lake Reservoir. Our Watershed Falls Lake Rules Hillsborough is required to treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because it is in a watershed that drains into Falls Lake. The lake is a 12,000-acre reservoir in Durham, Granville and Wake counties. It is on the Environmental Protection Agency’s list of impaired waters and has been designated nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. To protect and restore the lake’s water quality, the state created the Falls Lake Rules to\xa0reduce the amount of nitrogen and phosphorous that enter Falls Lake through rivers and streams.\xa0The first stage of the wastewater discharge rules went into effect in 2016, and the second stage is expected in 2036. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule. Additional upgrades to the Wastewater Treatment Plant likely will be required to meet the Stage 2 wastewater rules. Read more about the rules from the Upper Neuse River Basin Association . Nitrogen and Phosphorus Removal Hillsborough's wastewater treatment work helps protect the Eno River, which is classified as nutrient sensitive. The town is dedicated to maintaining the high water quality of our river and has successfully reduced the total nitrogen discharged into the river by approximately 85% since 2004. In 2022, the town discharged into the Eno River only 57.6% of the allowed total nitrogen and 56.7% of the allowed total phosphorus. The allowances are provided by the Falls Lake Rules. Wastewater Treatment Our Treated Wastewater Quality The town's wastewater treatment must meet extremely high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than\xa0220\xa0substances in the town's wastewater and solids and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Wastewater Quality Report . Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has a\xa0laboratory to perform frequent checks on the progress of wastewater treatment. Staff also test various points in the Eno River.  Tests occur: Upstream of the water intake Before and after wastewater discharge Downstream of wastewater discharge Our Wastewater Treatment Process The plant uses an enhanced biological nutrient removal process to treat wastewater.\xa0Naturally occurring nutrients can cause algae growth, depleted oxygen, and fish kills. The nutrients\xa0— like nitrogen and\xa0phosphorus — must be\xa0brought to safe levels before treated wastewater can be discharged into the Eno River. Treatment Steps The Town of Hillsborough's wastewater treatment plant Large item removal — Bar screens remove trash, wood and rocks, which then are compacted for disposal. Grit removal — Sand and dirt are removed for disposal. Secondary treatment — Organic materials and nutrients are removed through a series of aeration tanks and sent to large tanks called digesters (see 4). The remaining wastewater moves to the clarifiers (see 5). Solids treatment — Organic materials and nutrients are treated in digesters and then thickened and trucked to a composting facility to be turned into a soil amendment. Clarification — Rakes slowly skim the wastewater surface for scum like grease, oils, plastics and soap. The scum is pumped to digesters (see 4). Solids settle and are removed for disposal. Sand filtration — The remaining water moves through 6 feet of sand, which removes additional solids. Disinfection, final treatment — Chlorine is added to kill bacteria. Chemicals are added to neutralize the chlorine. Aeration — The water is re-aerated before discharge to remove dissolved gases and to oxidize dissolved metals. Discharge — The treated wastewater is returned to the Eno River at an outfall. Wastewater Treatment Plant and Tours Wastewater Treatment Plant employees are highly certified and operate and maintain the plant to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of wastewater per day.\xa0It was built in 1977, with upgrades in 2001 and\xa02007 to improve solids management, nutrient removal and\xa0treatment capabilities. The most recent expansion and upgrade was completed in 2014, allowing the plant to meet state\xa0requirements to protect\xa0Falls Lake, where Hillsborough’s watershed drains. This upgrade Hillsborough’s discharged water\xa0quality among the best in the nation. Awards In previous years, the Wastewater Treatment Plant and its staff have won awards for: Professionalism in the Operation of a Wastewater Treatment Plant Solutions to Operational Problems Wastewater Treatment Plant Operations and Maintenance Wastewater Treatment Plant Operator Tours See what it takes to treat used water for return to the Eno River. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require a completed request form. Request a Tour Wastewater Collection and System Maintenance About Pump Stations A pump station is a collection chamber that uses pumps to lift wastewater from a lower elevation to a higher elevation, where the wastewater can flow by gravity. It pumps into a pressurized main called a force main, which ends in a sewer manhole. There, wastewater can flow by gravity to the Wastewater Treatment Plant. The pump stations require frequent maintenance and weekly inspections. Improperly disposed rags, wipes or other trash can cause expensive repairs. Failures can result in larger sanitary sewer overflows than from a gravity sewer, with subsequent fines from the state. Why Pump Stations? Hillsborough has more pump stations per customer than surrounding water and sewer systems because of the town's terrain. Also, the systems were likely preferred decades ago due to the low upfront costs. Some of the pump stations were built by developers and turned over to the town to own and operate. Blocked Sewer Lines The town\xa0assesses sewer blockages to determine the cause and location — whether they are\xa0on the utility or property owner's side\xa0of a\xa0sewer line. Customers may be responsible for the cost of investigation and repairs if their\xa0 actions cause a blockage in the town’s sewer main. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the sewer main or sewer service line in public rights of ways or easements. It will clear blockages of lines in public rights of ways or easements. The town may require property owners to install or relocate cleanouts to the town's specifications. The town may agree to perform the work but may require reimbursement and the granting of property access from the customer. Property owners are responsible for blockages in the sewer service line between the house or structure on their property and the public right of way or easement.\xa0The town can require installation or relocation of a cleanout at the right of way or easement if one is not present. Sewer Cleanout A sewer cleanout is a capped pipe that allows access to the sewer line for maintenance and inspections. Cleanouts typically indicate the ownership line when located at the\xa0edge of the public right of way or easement. Sewer Maintenance The town’s primary responsibility for the sewer system is to protect the environment by maintaining unobstructed wastewater flow in the collection system’s main lines. Crews routinely do preventative maintenance and repairs, remove roots and clean sewer lines with pressure washers. Every year, staff is required by state mandate to hydraulically clean a minimum of 10% of the gravity sewer lines in the system. Smoke Testing Crews use non-toxic, non-staining vapor resembling smoke to identify leaks in the sewer system. During testing, it is normal to see smoke escaping from roof vents and from sinks or bathtubs that are not used frequently. If smoke appears from the sink or bathtub, try running water down the drain to fill the dry trap. If you see smoke indoors that is not due to a dry trap or that appears to be arising from the ground in your yard, call the Hillsborough Water Distribution and Collection Division at 919-296-9650 to have the problem investigated. The town will notify customers of private connection issues that require repair. The town will address issues with public sewer lines. For town and customers responsibilities, see the town Code of Ordinances, sections 14-21 and 14-22 . For more information, contact the utility maintenance supervisor at 919-296-9652. Wastewater Collection Division The Wastewater Collection Division cleans and repairs sewer lines and maintains sewage lift or pump stations. The town has: 100.82 miles of sewer lines, which includes: 84.06 miles of gravity sewer lines 16.76 miles of force main sewer lines 27 public pump stations 2,163 sewer manholes Awards In previous years, the Wastewater Collections Division and its staff have won awards from the N.C. American Water Works Association-Water Environment Association for: Outstanding Wastewater Collection Operator of the Year Small Wastewater Collection System of the Year Infrastructure Protection Fats, Oils and Grease Fats, oil and grease poured down the drain will collect and stick to plumbing and sewer lines. Over time, lines can become blocked, causing sewer backups or overflows. Properties that discharge greasy wastewater are required to have interceptors for fats, oil and grease. Residential customers can obtain a free fat trapper from the town. They also should: Discard\xa0used fats, oil\xa0and grease with other garbage or recycle\xa0at an Orange County solid waste convenience center. Avoid\xa0or limit\xa0use of garbage disposals. Scrape\xa0plates into trash cans. Use\xa0paper towels to wipe leftover oils and fats from pots and pans. Fat Trappers The town provides fat trappers to water and sewer customers to help keep grease and oils out of the sewer system. To receive a fat trapper or additional bags, contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor or visit the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St., where water bills are paid. Video Fats, Oils and Grease Program No Wipes in the Pipes Only waste and toilet paper should be flushed. Household cleaning and personal hygiene products labeled as flushable or safe for sewer or septic systems do not dissolve. Additionally, do not dispose of trash, medicine or other items in the sewer system. These items clog sewer lines and can damage sewer pumps and other equipment. For more information, contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Video No Wipes in the Pipes Contact Information Wastewater Collection Division Adron Thompson Water/Sewer Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Wastewater Treatment Plant, 355 Elizabeth Brady Road 919-296-9670 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Name: 40_vehicle_operating_employees_wireless
Representative_Docs: ['General Guidelines Even when not strictly prohibited below, employees are strongly encouraged to bring vehicles they are operating to a complete stop if they need to use electronic wireless communication devices or engage in any activity that may distract their driving. Employees may use electronic wireless communication devices any time a vehicle they are operating is stopped or when they are a passenger in a vehicle. Supervisors and employees shall refrain from communicating any critical information by text message or email to any employee who may be operating a vehicle. Police radios shall be used for communicating critical information. Critical information is information that is expected to be read or responded to in less than 30 minutes. Employees will not be penalized for reasonable delays in reading and/or responding to a text or email or in answering or returning phone calls they receive while operating a vehicle.', 'This policy establishes practices that reduce or eliminate distractions while Police Department employees are operating town-owned vehicles which are in motion. The department recognizes the inherent danger that exists when employees operate vehicles while distracted. The use of cellular phones, computers or other electronic devices while operating a vehicle can: Cause unnecessary distractions. Present a negative image to the public. Interfere with the safe operation of a vehicle. Impede response to calls for service. Comment on Policy A. Definitions Distracted Driving This is any activity that could divert a person’s attention from the primary task of driving. Employees who use a cell phone while driving expose themselves and others to a significant safety risk. Studies on cellular phones related vehicular accidents reveal that those individuals using cell phones while driving were significantly more likely to have an accident than those not using cell phones. Electronic Wireless Communication Devices These are any devices that can be used to send or receive information and/or communicate wirelessly. This includes cellular phones, smart phones, laptops, global positioning system (GPS) devices or tablets, regardless of whether they are provided by the town or personally owned. B. General Guidelines General Guidelines Even when not strictly prohibited below, employees are strongly encouraged to bring vehicles they are operating to a complete stop if they need to use electronic wireless communication devices or engage in any activity that may distract their driving. Employees may use electronic wireless communication devices any time a vehicle they are operating is stopped or when they are a passenger in a vehicle. Supervisors and employees shall refrain from communicating any critical information by text message or email to any employee who may be operating a vehicle. Police radios shall be used for communicating critical information. Critical information is information that is expected to be read or responded to in less than 30 minutes. Employees will not be penalized for reasonable delays in reading and/or responding to a text or email or in answering or returning phone calls they receive while operating a vehicle. C. Prohibitions While Operating a Vehicle Prohibitions While Operating a Vehicle While operating a vehicle in motion (including a town vehicle whether on or off duty and a town or personally owned vehicle for town business), employees shall not: Use a cellular phone in any mode other than hands-free nor for any purpose that is not strictly work related. View, search or otherwise try to manage body-worn or in-car videos. Eat, read printed material, perform personal grooming, smoke or engage in any other activity that significantly limits the use of one or both hands and is not directly work related and/or cannot be avoided until their vehicle comes to a complete stop. Engage in writing, sending, searching, replying, using an application (app) or reading any text-based communication on electronic wireless communication devices. Employees shall not alter, change or disengage any device designed to reduce distractions that is installed in a town vehicle. D. Exceptions Exceptions The following are exempt from these provisions: Operation and use of portable and vehicle-mounted radios and radio detection and ranging (RADAR) units. The activation and deactivation of in-car and body worn video cameras. Additionally: Employees may use a mobile data computer while in motion when one-button or one-touch responses are needed to activate an emergency button or to indicate an officer is en route or arriving on scene. Using a wireless communication device in a manner inconsistent with the prohibitions in Section C (Prohibitions While Operating a Vehicle) is permitted only when emergency or significant circumstances with a justification for immediacy exist and no reasonable alternative is available. Employees may not use any electronic wireless communication devices (other than a police radio and activation of in-car or body-worn cameras) when operating a vehicle that has lights and/or siren activated or when operating outside the normally expected rules of the road.', 'Even when not strictly prohibited below, employees are strongly encouraged to bring vehicles they are operating to a complete stop if they need to use electronic wireless communication devices or engage in any activity that may distract their driving. Employees may use electronic wireless communication devices any time a vehicle they are operating is stopped or when they are a passenger in a vehicle. Supervisors and employees shall refrain from communicating any critical information by text message or email to any employee who may be operating a vehicle. Police radios shall be used for communicating critical information. Critical information is information that is expected to be read or responded to in less than 30 minutes. Employees will not be penalized for reasonable delays in reading and/or responding to a text or email or in answering or returning phone calls they receive while operating a vehicle.']

Name: 42_business_occupation_checklist_starting
Representative_Docs: ["Checklist of Steps to Open a Business Below are steps that must be taken when starting, expanding or relocating a business. See the checklist for detailed information. Determine Zoning District ― Get help from economic development staff in determining the zoning district most appropriate for your\xa0business type\xa0before you sign a lease, purchase a building or enter into a land\xa0agreement. Select Possible Sites ― Economic development staff can suggest possible sites for your business. The town works\xa0closely with the Orange County Economic Development Office to keep\xa0an updated inventory of available sites. Meet about Development and Occupancy ―\xa0Hillsborough economic development staff will assist you in setting up a meeting with town and Orange County staff to discuss property development, new construction or the upfit of space to be leased. The town contracts with the county for building inspection services. Items to be discussed include: Compliance with the\xa0Americans with Disabilities Act, building codes and\xa0Health Department requirements for restaurants and food service\xa0establishments. Requirements for\xa0parking, lighting, landscaping, grading, grease traps, backflow\xa0prevention, fire access and fire suppression. Seek Permitting and Inspections ― Apply for all the\xa0permits necessary to start work on a space or to occupy the space once you've\xa0selected a site or existing building.\xa0These permits will have been discussed more extensively in the\xa0predevelopment meeting. Close the Project ― Economic development staff offer an optional meeting with business owners and developers at a project's conclusion to\xa0identify issues or challenges that may have impacted the project’s budget or timeline. The\xa0confidential meetings help improve the town's development processes. Name: 43_tree_trees_arbor_invasive
Representative_Docs: ["Invasive Plants Removal The invasive species removal team meets weekly to remove invasive plants from Hillsborough’s public spaces as part of a program of the Hillsborough Tree Board. The team gathers on Wednesdays for two hours in the afternoon in the cooler months and in the morning during warmer months. If it’s removal season, they bring loppers and shears to remove plants. If it’s planting season, they bring shovels and trowels. Trees Are Good The International Society of Arboriculture manages the educational website Trees Are Good, which offers quality tree care information for homeowners and other tree owners. The site helps increase awareness of the benefits of trees and provides resources to help sustain trees in an urban environment. Check out the directory to find an arborist and see the Tree Owner Resources for information, including why to hire an arborist and why topping hurts trees. Tree City USA The town has been a part of the Arbor Day Foundation's Tree City USA program since 2003. To qualify for the program, the town is required to have: A tree board or department. A tree care ordinance. A community forestry program with an annual budget of at least $2 per resident. An annual Arbor Day observance and proclamation. Name: 44_rinse_washing_dishes_save
Representative_Docs: ['In the Kitchen and Laundry Reduce the use of running water: Keep water in the refrigerator for drinking instead of running the faucet until water gets cold. Rinse vegetables in a pan of water. Defrost frozen foods in the microwave or refrigerator. Use the garbage disposal less. Consider composting. Putting food scraps and grease and oils down kitchen sinks risks clogging sewer pipes. Fill one sink with soapy water and one with rinse water when washing dishes by hand. If you have\xa0only one sink, use a dish rack and rinse with hot water. Use the smallest amount of detergent possible when washing dishes by hand. This reduces the amount of rinse water needed and the risk of clogged pipes. Use your dishwasher and washing machine for full loads only. If considering the replacement of large appliances: Select high-efficiency washing machines. Consider that new dishwasher models are more water- and energy-efficient.', 'Reduce the use of running water: Keep water in the refrigerator for drinking instead of running the faucet until water gets cold. Rinse vegetables in a pan of water. Defrost frozen foods in the microwave or refrigerator. Use the garbage disposal less. Consider composting. Putting food scraps and grease and oils down kitchen sinks risks clogging sewer pipes. Fill one sink with soapy water and one with rinse water when washing dishes by hand. If you have\xa0only one sink, use a dish rack and rinse with hot water. Use the smallest amount of detergent possible when washing dishes by hand. This reduces the amount of rinse water needed and the risk of clogged pipes. Use your dishwasher and washing machine for full loads only. If considering the replacement of large appliances: Select high-efficiency washing machines. Consider that new dishwasher models are more water- and energy-efficient.', 'In the Bathroom Reduce the amount of water used: Wet your toothbrush and then turn off the faucet. Rinse your razor in a partially filled sink instead of under a running tap. Shorten your shower. Cutting out one or two minutes can save up to 700 gallons of water each\xa0month. Take a shallow bath. This saves more water than a short shower. Install low-flow showerheads or flow restrictors. Install ultra-low flush toilets and faucets if building or remodeling a home. Reduce the amount of water flushed: Flush only when necessary and put trash in the trash can. Each flush wastes 5 to 7 gallons of water, and flushed trash can clog pipes. Put a filled plastic bottle in your toilet tank. Fill the bottle with water and a\xa0small amount of pebbles to weigh it down. Place it away from operating mechanisms. This can save you 10 or more gallons of water each day. Save water for other use: Place a bucket under the bath faucet when waiting for water to warm. Use the retained cold water\xa0later for house plants or to refill the toilet tank after flushing. Use water from pet dishes or dehumidifiers to water plants. In the Kitchen and Laundry Reduce the use of running water: Keep water in the refrigerator for drinking instead of running the faucet until water gets cold. Rinse vegetables in a pan of water. Defrost frozen foods in the microwave or refrigerator. Use the garbage disposal less. Consider composting. Putting food scraps and grease and oils down kitchen sinks risks clogging sewer pipes. Fill one sink with soapy water and one with rinse water when washing dishes by hand. If you have\xa0only one sink, use a dish rack and rinse with hot water. Use the smallest amount of detergent possible when washing dishes by hand. This reduces the amount of rinse water needed and the risk of clogged pipes. Use your dishwasher and washing machine for full loads only. If considering the replacement of large appliances: Select high-efficiency washing machines. Consider that new dishwasher models are more water- and energy-efficient. Outside and Dining Out If possible for outside plantings: Reduce or eliminate watering grass, bushes and plants. Reduce or eliminate planting new plants. If possible for outside maintenance: Avoid washing vehicles at home. Most commercial car washes recycle the wash water. Soapy\xa0runoff is not good for the environment or stormwater system. Consider delaying projects that use a lot of water, such as: Carpet cleaning with your own steam cleaner. Draining and refilling a hot water heater. Filling ponds, hot tubs, fountains or pools. Masonry projects that need a water mix for grout or concrete. Painting projects that require washing brushes, rollers and trays. Power washing. When dining out, if you: Won’t be drinking water, ask that no tap water be served. Will be drinking water, ask for bottled water during water restrictions.']

Name: 45_adjustment_leak_meter_customer
Representative_Docs: ['Customers may request a bill adjustment if a water line break has resulted in significantly greater charges for a billing period. One of the following is required: A plumber or maintenance person must provide sufficient documentation of a leak. Customers must provide receipts for items purchased for repairs. Customers must request leak adjustments no later than two months after the billing date for the adjustment. Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a 12-month period. If an adjustment is approved, the customer will be charged an amount based on an average of the previous six months. Leak Adjustment Request Leak Adjustment Request (Español)', "The Financial Services Department: Starts, stops and transfers billing accounts. Reads and maintains water meters. Performs service disconnections. Collects monthly payments. Emergencies and Alerts Give or Receive Aid Pay Online or By Phone Rates Start or Stop Service Tampering and Theft Fees and Charges Schedule Bill Adjustments Call customer service at 919-296-9450 for assistance. Bill adjustments will not be made if a meter or service connection has been tampered with. Leak Adjustment Customers may request a bill adjustment if a water line break has resulted in significantly greater charges for a billing period. One of the following is required: A plumber or maintenance person must provide sufficient documentation of a leak. Customers must provide receipts for items purchased for repairs. Customers must request leak adjustments no later than two months after the billing date for the adjustment. Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a 12-month period. If an adjustment is approved, the customer will be charged an amount based on an average of the previous six months. Leak Adjustment Request Leak Adjustment Request (Español) Malfunctioning Water Meter If a meter fails to register water use, the customer will be charged an amount for water and sewer usage based on an average of the previous six months. The standard for meter accuracy is plus or minus 10%. A meter flow test can be requested, with a fee charged if the meter passes the flow test. Contact Customer Service Billing Water and sewer usage are billed monthly. Customer Information Ensure your customer information is up to date. Please update your information with the town whenever you have a change in: Billing address Email address Telephone number Customer Information Update Due Date Your bill should arrive by mail during the first week of the month. Your payment due date for current charges is the 25th day of each month, with payment due the following business day if the due date falls on a weekend or holiday.\xa0Payments received after 11:59 p.m. on the due date are late. Meter Readings The town has about 7,000 water meters in its service area and two employees responsible for the meters. They: Read, repair, replace and test meters. Connect and disconnect water service. Meter information is gathered electronically from the employees' vehicles on a monthly basis. The employees will physically check meters that produce unusual readings, such as high, low or no usage. Service Disconnection Service is disconnected one month after payment has not been received (the previous month’s bill must be paid by the current month’s due date). To have service reconnected, pay the full balance and all penalties and fees. Fees and Charges Schedule How to Pay Bills The town encourages paying online or by telephone at\xa0833-262-5902\xa0with credit or debit card. Both options are available 24 hours a day. Bank Draft Have the town automatically draft payment from your bank account on the 10th or 25th day of each month. Submit the form with a voided check by mail, in person or by deposit\xa0box. Pay by other methods until your bill states, “Do not pay. Paid by draft.”\xa0Enrollment takes at least one billing cycle. Forms Start Bank Draft Automated Debit Service Enrollment Form Automated Debit Service Enrollment Form (Español) Stop Bank Draft Request to Discontinue Automated Debit Service Request to Discontinue Automated Debit Service (Español) Bank Originated Bill Pay Set up recurring payments through your bank account. Starting electronic payments from your bank can take up to 90 days. Your bank will issue a paper check for payment during this setup period. Log on to your bank's online bill pay service and enter Town of Hillsborough as a payee with this “remit to” address: Town of Hillsborough, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Enter your account or customer number in the format shown on your bill. Deposit Box, In Person or Mail Deposit Box — Leave non-cash payments in the box at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. In Person — Make cash or check payments or get staff help with credit or debit card payments between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m. weekdays\xa0at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Mail — Send payments to Town of Hillsborough, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Online Payments Site Registration To use the online features, v isit the town payments and donations website to register. Account numbers are provided on bills. To confirm your registration on the website, you will need your account number and the amount you last paid for water or sewer charges. Contact customer service for the amount to enter if you do not have your last paid bill. Benefits Utility customers who register to use the online platform can: View utility bills online. Set up automatic payments from debit or credit cards. Sign up for text alerts. Register for bills by email. They also: Have access to their account information around the clock daily, including usage history and monthly readings. Have ability to print current and previous bills. Do not have to remember their last payment amount to make a payment. Can pay electronically, potentially saving time and avoiding late fees. Fees The town does not add any fees or surcharges to electronic payments of utility bills at this time. Automatic bank draft is a convenient payment method that generates no fees for customers and minimal fees for the town. Contact Information Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Office Hours —\xa09 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday. 919-296-9450, Option 2 Contact Customer Service Staff Title Phone Email Daye, Linda Billing and Customer Service Supervisor 919-296-9462 Email", 'Leak Adjustment Customers may request a bill adjustment if a water line break has resulted in significantly greater charges for a billing period. Name: 46_animal_animals_destroying_destroy
Representative_Docs: ['Before destroying a badly injured domestic animal, the officer shall make reasonable efforts to notify the animal’s owner and/or any appropriate agencies or authorities to involve more knowledgeable persons in the decision. Appropriate agencies include Animal Control, the Humane Society or a nearby veterinary office. If no suitable alternative to destruction is identified, the officer may proceed with destroying animal upon supervisory approval.', 'Officers are authorized to use force when it is reasonably necessary to end a badly injured animal’s suffering. Using force to destroy an animal is not considered a use of force for reporting and tracking purposes. Officers are required to complete an incident report only in this instance. If officers are required to use force to defend themselves or another person against an immediate threat of significant physical injury from an animal, then all use of force procedures and processes from this general order apply. Before destroying an animal, officers must notify their supervisor of the situation and their intentions. No weapon shall be fired to destroy any wild or domestic animal unless the weapon can be fired safely with respect to human life and other property. Following destruction, the officer will be responsible for arranging the removal or other appropriate handling of the animal before leaving the scene. Before destroying a badly injured domestic animal, the officer shall make reasonable efforts to notify the animal’s owner and/or any appropriate agencies or authorities to involve more knowledgeable persons in the decision. Appropriate agencies include Animal Control, the Humane Society or a nearby veterinary office. If no suitable alternative to destruction is identified, the officer may proceed with destroying animal upon supervisory approval.', 'Use of Force to Destroy Animals Officers are authorized to use force when it is reasonably necessary to end a badly injured animal’s suffering. Using force to destroy an animal is not considered a use of force for reporting and tracking purposes. Officers are required to complete an incident report only in this instance. If officers are required to use force to defend themselves or another person against an immediate threat of significant physical injury from an animal, then all use of force procedures and processes from this general order apply. Before destroying an animal, officers must notify their supervisor of the situation and their intentions. No weapon shall be fired to destroy any wild or domestic animal unless the weapon can be fired safely with respect to human life and other property. Following destruction, the officer will be responsible for arranging the removal or other appropriate handling of the animal before leaving the scene. Before destroying a badly injured domestic animal, the officer shall make reasonable efforts to notify the animal’s owner and/or any appropriate agencies or authorities to involve more knowledgeable persons in the decision. Appropriate agencies include Animal Control, the Humane Society or a nearby veterinary office. If no suitable alternative to destruction is identified, the officer may proceed with destroying animal upon supervisory approval.']

Name: 47_security_home_garage_windows
Representative_Docs: ["On request and at no cost, officers will: Assess a home's security and offer suggestions for safety improvements related to lighting and landscaping. Provide security checks of homes in town limits when residents are not home. Home Security Checks Officers will: Check your home when you are away to ensure that doors and windows appear secure and untouched. Look for irregularities, such as damage to property and leaking pipes. They cannot guarantee that all irregularities will be found. Officers will not: Enter a home without signs that the home is unsecured. Water or cut grass. Feed animals. To request a house check, submit a Police Department contact form or contact the police sergeant or senior administrative support specialist. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether your home is in town limits.", 'Subscribe to news releases or sign up for emergency alerts . Police Conduct Home Security Checks Hillsborough police officers provide free home security checks and advice Post Date: May 27, 2026 8:30 a.m. Police Officer Jordan McDonald demonstrates a security check at a Hillsborough home. Whether you’re away for the day or for several weeks, the Hillsborough Police Department can help you make your home more secure. When requested, Hillsborough police officers will check on your home when you’re away and provide advice on how to make your property more secure before you go. “We want to do everything we can to help our residents,” said Police Sgt. Scott Foster, who leads the department’s community action team. “Our mission is to protect people and property.” According to a study by the home security company ADT, 65% of home break-ins occur during the day and more home burglaries occur in the summer than in other seasons. Asking the Police Department for help or following officers’ general tips may help save you the headache of a home break-in. Security and Property Checks When requested, an officer will visit your property as time permits and walk around the outside of your home. The time of the visit will be logged, and the visit will include visually checking that your windows and doors remain secure. For security assistance beyond two weeks, the Police Department offers a property check, which involves driving by your home as time permits but not necessarily walking on your property. In addition to giving you peace of mind, the frequent visits may deter someone from breaking in. They also provide a time frame for a break-in, should one occur. To schedule a security or property check, contact Senior Administrative Support Specialist Danielle King at least two business days before you plan to be away  at 919-296-9500 or by contact form . These services are secondary to primary emergency services but are still taken very seriously. A date, time or frequency cannot be guaranteed, but the Hillsborough Police Department does its best to maintain a high standard of customer service for these requests. Security Consultation Police officers also are available to meet with you to evaluate the security of your house and property and make suggestions. This consultation includes outside security considerations, such as fences, lighting and landscaping as well as doors, windows and locks. It also includes interior security, such as alarms, safes and how to best protect property inside the home. To request a security consultation, contact Foster by phone at 919-296-9563 or by contact form. General Security Tips Consider these simple tips to help secure your home. Outside: Lock up ladders — An unsecured ladder may be used to break into your home. Avoid hiding a key outside. Leave a key with a neighbor if you’re worried about locking yourself out. If you can, park your car in your garage while you’re away rather than in the driveway. Inside your home: Use a timer so lights come on at night, but don’t have only the hallway light on timer. Place a timer on a radio. Close blinds or drapes, or remove valuables from areas that can be seen through windows. In your garage: Lock garage doors. If your garage is attached to your house, lock the house door in your garage. Remove garage door opener remotes from any cars remaining on your property while you’re away. Other: Don’t post on social media that you’re on vacation. Test smoke alarms before going on vacation. Ask a neighbor to pick up your mail and packages. Return to full list >> Subscribe to News Subscribe to Emergency Alerts', "Home Security Assessments and Checks On request and at no cost, officers will: Assess a home's security and offer suggestions for safety improvements related to lighting and landscaping. Provide security checks of homes in town limits when residents are not home. Home Security Checks Officers will: Check your home when you are away to ensure that doors and windows appear secure and untouched. Look for irregularities, such as damage to property and leaking pipes. They cannot guarantee that all irregularities will be found. Officers will not: Enter a home without signs that the home is unsecured. Water or cut grass. Feed animals. To request a house check, submit a Police Department contact form or contact the police sergeant or senior administrative support specialist. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether your home is in town limits."]

Representative_Docs: ["Irrigation Systems and Permits See information about irrigation requirements and limitations on the town's Water Conservation page. Plumbing Permit The North Carolina Plumbing Code requires a permit from the local inspections department for installation of an irrigation system. The Orange County Planning and Inspections Department issues permits and performs inspections. Contact the department at 919-245-2600. Supplemental Irrigation Permit Permits for supplemental irrigation may be purchased to protect new plantings for 90 days. To be eligible, a property must have an active building permit or have had one within the previous 90 days. No permits are issued during water restrictions at Stage 2 or higher. For permit prices, see the town's Fees and Charges Schedule . Fees and Other Charges The Town of Hillsborough charges a system development fee to cover the cost of water supplies, treatment facilities, pumps, piping and other infrastructure necessary to provide service to an irrigation-only meter. The fee is the same as that for a regular water service, but sewer fees are not charged. Irrigation-only water service is charged monthly per 1,000 gallons of water use. The town may halt irrigation-only service during water shortages, such as due to droughts. If the service is halted, the town will bill only for the actual amount of water used during the affected months. It will not charge a minimum fee during those months. Any changes to rates and fees will occur typically at the start of a new fiscal year, which is July 1. Name: 49_pipes_pipe_open_valve
Representative_Docs: ['Use these tips to protect water pipes during freezing temperatures. Unheated Areas Pipes in unheated areas have the greatest chance of freezing. Unheated areas include attics, basements and crawl spaces. Insulate pipes ― Pipe insulation is available in fiberglass or foam sleeves.\xa0Home centers and hardware stores have sleeves providing one-eighth to five-eighths inches of insulation. Specialty dealers have products that provide up to 2 inches of insulation. The extra thickness can save a pipe. Heat pipes ―\xa0Heating cables and tapes are effective. Select a heating cable with the UL label and a built-in thermostat that turns\xa0the heat on when needed. Follow the manufacturer’s instructions closely. If you have it, turn on an incandescent\xa0light bulb in your crawl space for extra warmth. Seal openings and air leaks ― This includes access doors, air vents in the foundation, and cracks.\xa0Especially keep cold wind away from pipes, which speeds the freezing process. Close and seal cracks and other openings with insulation, cardboard, plastic or newspaper. Foundation air vents may have hinged or sliding covers to easily close the vents. Ensure your water meter box cover is closed ― The cover prevents cold air from freezing water inside the meter. If the cover is broken or missing and you are a Hillsborough water customer, call the Town of Hillsborough at 919-732-3621. Heated Areas Leave cabinet doors open under kitchen and bathroom sinks ― This\xa0allows warmer room air to\xa0circulate around pipes. Let water drip slowly from the highest faucet in the building ― Use this tip if you have difficulty protecting pipes in an unheated area or if you believe your pipes may freeze for other reasons. An open faucet allows water to escape before pressure builds to a point where a pipe can burst. If dripping stops, ice may be blocking the pipe. Keep the\xa0faucet open because the pipe still needs pressure relief.', 'Preventing Frozen and Burst Pipes Use these tips to protect water pipes during freezing temperatures. Close and seal cracks and other openings with insulation, cardboard, plastic or newspaper. Foundation air vents may have hinged or sliding covers to easily close the vents. Ensure your water meter box cover is closed ― The cover prevents cold air from freezing water inside the meter. If the cover is broken or missing and you are a Hillsborough water customer, call the Town of Hillsborough at 919-732-3621. Let water drip slowly from the highest faucet in the building ― Use this tip if you have difficulty protecting pipes in an unheated area or if you believe your pipes may freeze for other reasons. An open faucet allows water to escape before pressure builds to a point where a pipe can burst. If dripping stops, ice may be blocking the pipe. Keep the\xa0faucet open because the pipe still needs pressure relief. Frozen and Burst Pipes If Your Pipes Are Frozen If nothing comes out of your faucets: Leave the faucets open and call a plumber. Try to thaw the pipe with warm air from a hair dryer ― Start by warming the pipe as close to the faucet as possible. Then work toward the coldest section of pipe. Do not use electrical appliances in areas of standing water. You could be electrocuted. Do not use a torch or other open flame. This could cause a fire hazard. If Your Pipes Have Burst Turn off water at the main shutoff valve and leave faucets open\xa0― Make sure everyone knows where the master valve is for your plumbing system and how to open and close it. The valve may be in your basement, crawl space or closet where water pipes enter the house or building. If you do not have a master shutoff valve,\xa0consider installing one. Drain the pipes in your plumbing system ― Open the highest and lowest cold-water faucets in your house until the water is depleted. Before draining pipes\xa0― Turn off the water heater to ensure the lack of water does not burn out the\xa0heating elements. After draining pipes ―\xa0Before turning on the water heater again, ensure water service is restored and the water tank is refilled. If you fix a leak, you may qualify for an adjustment to your utility bill. See the Billing and Adjustments page.']
